title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text: Develops the Cayley–Hamilton theorem \(p(M) = 0\) — every matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial — in four logically distinct ways. (1) Pure algebraic via the adjugate identity (already proved, recapped). (2) Cyclic basis (the session’s working proof): build a basis \(\mathbf{v}_1, M\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, M^{n-1}\mathbf{v}_1\), express \(M^n\mathbf{v}_1\) as a linear combination — the coefficients turn out to be the characteristic-polynomial coefficients. (3) Generalised eigenvector / Jordan block (sketched, deferred). (4) Exterior volume (the teacher’s own unconventional proof, sketched). The session completes (1) and (2), and flags the existence gap in (2): not every matrix admits a cyclic basis (e.g., the identity).

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions

Characteristic polynomial \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^n - a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} - \cdots - a_0\).
Adjugate identity \(A \cdot \operatorname{adj}(A) = \operatorname{adj}(A) \cdot A = \det(A) \cdot I\) (2026-03-07 cramer-review Thm 8).
Polynomials in one matrix commute: \(f(M) \cdot g(M) = g(M) \cdot f(M)\).
Linear map by basis: \(T = 0\) iff \(T\mathbf{v}_i = 0\) for all basis vectors.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Theorems

(Cayley–Hamilton). For any \(M \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), \[
p(M) \;=\; 0. \tag{58}
\]

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Derivation A — pure algebraic (recapped)
Adjugate identity on \(A = \lambda I - M\): \[
(\lambda I - M) \cdot \operatorname{adj}(\lambda I - M) \;=\; p(\lambda) \cdot I. \tag{59}
\] \(\operatorname{adj}(\lambda I - M)\) is a polynomial in \(\lambda\) of degree \(n-1\) with matrix-valued coefficients in \(M\) alone (so they all commute with \(M\)): \[
\operatorname{adj}(\lambda I - M) = \lambda^{n-1}B_{n-1} + \cdots + B_0.
\] Matching coefficients of \(\lambda^k\) in (59) gives \(n+1\) matrix identities. Multiplying the \(k\)-th by \(M^k\) and adding all — the LHS telescopes to \(p(M)\), RHS sums to \(0\). Hence \(p(M) = 0\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)






Note



Commutativity is load-bearing. The telescoping requires \(M\) and each \(B_k\) to commute. Toby’s exchange: “It’s basically the inverse?” — corrected by Emma: “Adjugate is defined whether or not the inverse exists. Verify by direct cofactor multiplication.” Also, every \(B_k\) is a polynomial in \(M\) alone, so commutativity is automatic.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
section: Derivation B — cyclic basis (the session’s working path)
text:
Derivation B — cyclic basis (the session’s working path)
Setup. Pick \(\mathbf{v}_1\) so that \(\{\mathbf{v}_1, M\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, M^{n-1}\mathbf{v}_1\}\) is a basis (the existence gap — see Fragility).
Step 1 — linear-dependence. \(M^n\mathbf{v}_1\) is a linear combination of the basis: \[
M^n \mathbf{v}_1 \;=\; c_{n-1}M^{n-1}\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_1 M\mathbf{v}_1 + c_0 \mathbf{v}_1. \tag{60}
\]
Step 2 — matrix of \(M\) in cyclic basis. With \(\mathbf{v}_k := M^{k-1}\mathbf{v}_1\): \[
M\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{v}_{k+1} \;\; (k<n), \quad M\mathbf{v}_n = c_{n-1}\mathbf{v}_n + \cdots + c_0\mathbf{v}_1.
\] \[
[M]_{\text{cyclic}} = \begin{pmatrix}0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & c_0\\1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & c_1\\0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & c_2\\\vdots & & \ddots & & \vdots\\0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & c_{n-1}\end{pmatrix}. \tag{61}
\]
Step 3 — characteristic poly of companion form. Cofactor expansion of \(\det(\lambda I - [M]_{\text{cyclic}})\) along the last column gives: \[
p(\lambda) = \lambda^n - c_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} - \cdots - c_0. \tag{62}
\] Matching with \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^n - a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} - \cdots - a_0\): \[
c_i \;=\; a_i. \tag{63}
\]
Step 4 — Cayley–Hamilton on seed vector. Substitute (63) into (60), rearrange: \[
p(M)\mathbf{v}_1 = M^n\mathbf{v}_1 - \sum_i a_i M^i\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}.
\]
Step 5 — extend to basis. For \(k \ge 2\), \(\mathbf{v}_k = M^{k-1}\mathbf{v}_1\), so \[
p(M)\mathbf{v}_k = p(M)M^{k-1}\mathbf{v}_1 = M^{k-1}p(M)\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}.
\] \(p(M)\) vanishes on a basis ⇒ \(p(M) = 0\). \(\quad\blacksquare\) (modulo gap)

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
section: Derivation C — generalised eigenvector / Jordan block (sketched)
text:
Derivation C — generalised eigenvector / Jordan block (sketched)
For \(M = VJV^{-1}\) with \(J\) block-diagonal Jordan blocks \(J_k = \lambda_k I_{m_k} + N_k\): \(p(\lambda) = \prod_k(\lambda - \lambda_k)^{m_k}\), \(p(M) = V\,p(J)\,V^{-1}\), and \(p(J)\) is block-diagonal whose \(k\)-th block contains \((J_k - \lambda_k I)^{m_k} = N_k^{m_k} = 0\). So \(p(M) = 0\). (Uses general Jordan canonical form — deferred.)

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Derivation D — exterior volume (sketched)
Uses \(a_k = \operatorname{tr}(\wedge^k M)\) on the \(\binom{n}{k}\)-dim representation. Then \(p(M) = 0\) follows from a higher-volume vanishing identity. (Teacher’s own unconventional proof; deferred.)

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Verification audit


\(n=1\). \(M = (m)\), \(p(M) = M - mI = 0\). ✓
\(n=2\). \(M = \begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\), \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + (ad-bc)\). \(p(M)_{11} = a^2 + bc - (a+d)a + (ad-bc) = 0\). ✓ Other entries similarly.
Defective Jordan block. \(M = \begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\), \(p(M) = (M-2I)^2 = \begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}^2 = 0\). ✓
Identity matrix. \(M = I\), \(p(M) = (I-I)^n = 0\). ✓ Note: cyclic-basis proof would FAIL here (\(M\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}\) ⇒ basis collapses to span of \(\mathbf{v}\)); Derivation A or C still works.
Dependency. A: adjugate + telescoping. B: cyclic basis + companion-matrix determinant. C: Jordan + nilpotency. No circularity; complementary.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Key frames



Frame @ 05m00 — Four-proof roadmap: algebraic, cyclic basis, generalised eigenvector, exterior volume.





Frame @ 18m00 — Commutativity check: \(\operatorname{adj}(\lambda I - M)\) is a polynomial in \(M\), hence commutes with \(M\).





Frame @ 32m00 — Cyclic basis construction and companion-matrix form (61).





Frame @ 50m00 — \(c_i = a_i\) identification + existence gap flagged.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Teacher: “Why do \(\lambda I - M\) and \(\operatorname{adj}(\lambda I - M)\) commute?” Toby: “Because it’s basically the inverse?” Teacher: “Not always — only when \(\det \ne 0\).” Emma: “Because the adjugate is the cofactor matrix, and direct multiplication gives \(A \cdot \operatorname{adj}(A) = \operatorname{adj}(A) \cdot A = \det A \cdot I\) — defining property regardless of invertibility.”
Teaching move: commutativity is load-bearing in Derivation A. Two complementary verifications (direct adjugate computation + polynomial-in-\(M\) structure).


Teacher: “Why isn’t the cyclic-basis proof complete?” Teacher (self-answers): “Take \(M = I\). Every \(M^k\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{v}_1\) — cyclic basis collapses. The proof fails. Need different machinery for matrices without cyclic bases.”
Teaching move: explicit load-bearing assumption identification. Proof B is valid where it applies; the gap is the hypothesis.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework.

AI-assisted four-proof comparison: load-bearing imports, scope, applicability.
Show: \(M\) admits a cyclic basis iff its minimal polynomial equals its characteristic polynomial.
Verify Cayley–Hamilton for \(M = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 0\\0 & 3 & 1\\1 & 0 & 2\end{pmatrix}\) explicitly.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step. Cyclic-basis existence is not universal (\(M = I\) counterexample). Derivation B is valid for matrices with cyclic basis; combined with C, gives full coverage.
Derivation A is the safest. Pure algebra, no basis assumption. Standard textbook proof.
Off-topic content. ~70% of recording is methodological meta-discussion (parent-teacher meeting on AI-assisted learning). Math content concentrated in first ~15 minutes.
Forward references. Full Jordan canonical form (Derivation C) is deeper; the \(2 \times 2\) case is in Apr 4 morning.
Confidence. Derivation A: high. Derivation B: high modulo gap. Derivations C, D: sketched only.

title: Cayley–Hamilton via Four Proof Strategies: Algebraic, Cyclic Basis, Generalised Eigenvector, Exterior Volume

Precursors: 2026-03-07 cramer-review (adjugate identity); 2026-04-04 morning + afternoon (2) (Jordan blocks); 2026-02-28 followup (characteristic polynomial).
Settles forward reference from Apr 4 morning Theorem 53 (\((M - \lambda I)^2 = 0\) for double-root Jordan blocks — the \(n=2\) Cayley–Hamilton special case).

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text: Continues 2026-04-04 morning by explicitly computing the closed form \(a_n = (A + Bn)\lambda^n\) from \(\mathbf{a}_n = V(\lambda I + N)^n V^{-1}\mathbf{a}_0\). Generalises to \(k \times k\) Jordan blocks: \(N^j\) shifts ones to the \(j\)-th super-diagonal, \(N^k = 0\), and the binomial expansion of \((\lambda I + N)^n\) truncates after \(k\) terms, producing \(\lambda^n\) times a polynomial in \(n\) of degree \(k - 1\). Closes by introducing the rotation matrix \(R(\theta)\) as an example of a matrix that is not diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{R}\): its eigenvalues are complex \(e^{\pm i\theta}\), with no real-direction eigenvector.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation

Closes by introducing the rotation matrix \(R(\theta)\) as an example of a matrix that is not diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{R}\): its eigenvalues are complex \(e^{\pm i\theta}\), with no real-direction eigenvector.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Symbol dictionary








Symbol
Meaning




\(J = \lambda I + N\)
Jordan block (\(k \times k\)); \(\lambda\) on diagonal, 1’s on subdiagonal


\(N\)
nilpotent: \(N_{i+1,i} = 1\), all other entries 0; \(N^k = 0\)


\(\binom{n}{j}\)
binomial coefficient


\(R(\theta)\)
2D rotation matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\\sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{pmatrix}\)


\(e^{\pm i\theta}\)
complex eigenvalues of \(R(\theta)\)

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions

Jordan-block decomposition (morning Theorem 53).
Matrix binomial expansion requires the matrices to commute. \(\lambda I\) commutes with everything, so \((\lambda I + N)^n = \sum_j \binom{n}{j}\lambda^{n-j} N^j\).
Nilpotent shift behaviour: \(N^j\) has 1’s on the \(j\)-th sub-diagonal; \(N^k = 0\).
Euler’s formula: \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\).

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Theorems

(\(k \times k\) Jordan block power). For \(J = \lambda I + N\) with \(N\) the standard \(k \times k\) nilpotent: \[
J^n \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\binom{n}{j}\lambda^{n-j}\,N^j. \tag{55}
\]


(Closed form for \(k\)-fold-root linear recursion). A linear recursion with characteristic polynomial having a single root \(\lambda\) of multiplicity \(k\) has general solution \[
a_n \;=\; \lambda^n \cdot P(n), \qquad \deg P \le k - 1. \tag{56}
\] \(P(n) = A_0 + A_1 n + \cdots + A_{k-1}n^{k-1}\) has \(k\) free coefficients, fitting \(k\) initial conditions.


(Rotation matrix has complex eigenvalues). \(R(\theta)\) has characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^2 - 2\cos\theta\,\lambda + 1\), with roots \(\lambda_\pm = e^{\pm i\theta}\). Real eigenvalues only when \(\theta \in \pi\mathbb{Z}\). For generic \(\theta\), \(R(\theta)\) is not diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{R}\) — no real eigenvector exists.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Derivation of Theorem 55 (Jordan block power)
Commutativity. \(\lambda I\) commutes with \(N\) (scalar matrices commute with everything). The matrix binomial theorem applies: \[
J^n = (\lambda I + N)^n = \sum_{j=0}^{n}\binom{n}{j}\lambda^{n-j} N^j.
\]
Truncation. \(N^j = 0\) for \(j \ge k\), so the sum collapses to \(j = 0, \dots, k-1\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Explicit \(k = 4\) case (worked in recording): \[
(\lambda I + N)^n \;=\; \lambda^n\begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & 0 & 0\\ n/\lambda & 1 & 0 & 0\\ \binom{n}{2}/\lambda^2 & n/\lambda & 1 & 0\\ \binom{n}{3}/\lambda^3 & \binom{n}{2}/\lambda^2 & n/\lambda & 1\end{pmatrix}.
\] Each \(j\)-th subdiagonal entry is \(\binom{n}{j}\lambda^{n-j}\); pulling out \(\lambda^n\) yields the form above.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Derivation of Theorem 56 (polynomial-in-\(n\) closed form)
From \(\mathbf{a}_n = V J^n V^{-1} \mathbf{a}_0\), the first component (reading \(a_n\)) is a linear combination of \(\binom{n}{j}\lambda^{n-j}\) for \(j = 0, \dots, k-1\). Each \(\binom{n}{j}\) is a polynomial of degree \(j\) in \(n\), so the linear combination yields \(\lambda^n\) times a polynomial of degree \(\le k-1\): \[
a_n = \lambda^n \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A_j \binom{n}{j}\lambda^{-j} = \lambda^n \cdot P(n).
\] The \(k\) coefficients \(A_j\) are determined by the \(k\) initial conditions \(a_0, \dots, a_{k-1}\) via the Vandermonde-like linear system. \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Derivation of Theorem 57 (complex rotation eigenvalues)
\(R(\theta) - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta - \lambda & -\sin\theta\\\sin\theta & \cos\theta - \lambda\end{pmatrix}\). Determinant: \[
\det(R - \lambda I) = (\cos\theta - \lambda)^2 + \sin^2\theta = \lambda^2 - 2\cos\theta\,\lambda + 1.
\] Quadratic formula: \(\lambda_\pm = \cos\theta \pm \sqrt{\cos^2\theta - 1} = \cos\theta \pm i|\sin\theta| = e^{\pm i\theta}\) (using \(i^2 = -1\)). Discriminant \(\cos^2\theta - 1 < 0\) unless \(\theta \in \pi\mathbb{Z}\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Geometric meaning: \(R(\theta)\) preserves no real direction for \(\theta \notin \pi\mathbb{Z}\). Complex eigenvectors \(\mathbf{v}_\pm = (1, \mp i)^T\) live in \(\mathbb{C}^2\). Over \(\mathbb{C}\), \(R(\theta) = U \operatorname{diag}(e^{i\theta}, e^{-i\theta}) U^{-1}\). Over \(\mathbb{R}\), it isn’t diagonalisable.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Verification audit


\(N^2\) for \(k=3\). \(N = \begin{pmatrix}0&0&0\\1&0&0\\0&1&0\end{pmatrix}\). \(N^2 = \begin{pmatrix}0&0&0\\0&0&0\\1&0&0\end{pmatrix}\). \(N^3 = 0\). ✓
Truncation at \(k=2\), \(n=5\). \((λI+N)^5 = \lambda^5 I + 5\lambda^4 N + 0 + \cdots = \lambda^5 I + 5\lambda^4 N\). Matches morning Theorem 54. ✓
\(k=3\) closed form. Triple root \(\lambda\) gives \(a_n = \lambda^n(A_0 + A_1 n + A_2 n^2)\) — three free constants for \(a_0, a_1, a_2\). ✓
Rotation eigenvalue at \(\theta = \pi/2\). \(\lambda_\pm = \pm i\). Char poly \(\lambda^2 + 1\), roots \(\pm i\). ✓
Rotation at \(\theta = \pi\). \(\lambda_\pm = -1\) (double real root). Char poly \((\lambda+1)^2\). ✓ (\(R(\pi) = -I\) is trivially diagonal.)
Dependency check. Thm 55: binomial + nilpotent (clean). Thm 56: Thm 55 + linear combination + Vandermonde (imported). Thm 57: characteristic polynomial (clean). No circularity.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Key frames



Frame @ 08m00 — Recap of morning’s Jordan-block setup \(M = V(3I + N)V^{-1}\) for \(a_{n+1} = 6a_n - 9a_{n-1}\).





Frame @ 22m00 — Explicit binomial expansion of \((3I + N)^n\) truncated by \(N^2 = 0\); recovering \(a_n = (A + Bn)\cdot 3^n\).





Frame @ 38m00 — \(k \times k\) Jordan block generalisation: \(N^j\) shifts ones to \(j\)-th super-diagonal; explicit \(4 \times 4\) power formula.





Frame @ 52m00 — Pivot to rotation matrices; \(\lambda^2 - 2\cos\theta\lambda + 1\), complex eigenvalues \(e^{\pm i\theta}\), no real eigenvector for generic \(\theta\).

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Dependency map





flowchart TB
    A["Jordan-block J = λI + N<br/>(morning Thm 53)"] --> B["λI commutes with N<br/>⇒ binomial applies"]
    B --> C["J^n = Σⱼ C(n,j) λ^(n-j) N^j"]
    D["N^k = 0<br/>(nilpotent)"] --> E["Truncate at j = k-1<br/>Theorem 55"]
    C --> E

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Teacher: “\(4 \times 4\) Jordan block to the \(n\)-th power. What’s \(N^4\)?” Helms: “Zero.” Teacher: “Binomial truncates after \(k = 4\) terms. \(\lambda^n\) on diagonal, \(n\lambda^{n-1}\) on first sub, \(\binom{n}{2}\lambda^{n-2}\) on second, \(\binom{n}{3}\lambda^{n-3}\) on third.”
Teaching move: nilpotent shift makes the binomial truncate. The Pascal-triangle-like sub-diagonal coefficients are the same combinatorics as Lucas’s \(1/(1-u)^r\) hypothesis from Jan 24.


Teacher: “\(R(\theta)\) — eigenvalues?” Helms: “\(\cos\theta \pm i\sin\theta\).” Teacher: “\(e^{\pm i\theta}\). Complex. Geometrically?” Helms: “Rotation doesn’t keep any real direction.” Teacher: “Right. Eigenvectors live in \(\mathbb{C}^2\). Not diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{R}\).”
Teaching move: algebraic fact (complex eigenvalues) ↔︎ geometric fact (no preserved real direction).

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework.

Write out \((\lambda I + N)^n\) for \(k = 5\) — all \(5 \times 5\) entries.
Show: a \(k \times k\) Jordan block has only one eigenvector (1-dimensional eigenspace), regardless of \(k\).
For \(R(\theta)\) with \(\theta = \pi/3\), find complex eigenvectors and the change-of-basis \(U\) such that \(U^{-1}R(\theta)U = \operatorname{diag}(e^{i\theta}, e^{-i\theta})\). Verify.

title: k×k Jordan Block Powers via Nilpotent Binomial; Real Eigenvalues vs Complex Rotation
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step. Theorem 56’s claim that \(k\) free coefficients \(A_j\) uniquely fit \(k\) initial conditions requires non-degeneracy of a Vandermonde-like linear system — imported, not proved.
Matrix binomial theorem requires commutativity. \(\lambda I\) commutes with everything, so OK here.
Complex eigenvectors live in \(\mathbb{C}^2\). The real-Schur decomposition (block-diagonal with \(2 \times 2\) rotation blocks) provides a real treatment but is not derived here.
Confidence. Theorems 55, 57: high. Theorem 56: high with the Vandermonde import.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text: The session proves Cramer’s rule from the cofactor formula for \(M^{-1}\), then uses it together with a column-space argument to establish that left and right inverses of a square matrix coincide: if \(M^{-1}M=I\) then \(MM^{-1}=I\). The proof technique — upgrading “the matrix \(A\) vanishes on each of \(n\) linearly independent vectors” to “\(A=0\)” — generalises and recurs throughout the course. The lesson closes by defining a linear vector space as a set closed under linear combination, and proves that \(\ker M\) is one. The analogous claim for \(\operatorname{range}(M)\) is left as homework.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces

The proof technique — upgrading “the matrix \(A\) vanishes on each of \(n\) linearly independent vectors” to “\(A=0\)” — generalises and recurs throughout the course. The lesson closes by defining a linear vector space as a set closed under linear combination, and proves that \(\ker M\) is one. The analogous claim for \(\operatorname{range}(M)\) is left as homework.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Symbol dictionary








Symbol
Meaning




\(M \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\)
square real coefficient matrix; assumed invertible unless stated otherwise


\(m_{ij}\)
scalar entry of \(M\) in row \(i\), column \(j\)


\(\widetilde{M}_{ij}\)
\((n-1)\times(n-1)\) submatrix obtained by deleting row \(i\) and column \(j\)


\(C_{ij} := (-1)^{i+j}\det\widetilde{M}_{ij}\)
cofactor of entry \(m_{ij}\) (the transcript writes “\(M_{ji}\)” for this; we use \(C_{ij}\) to avoid index collision with the matrix itself)


\(\operatorname{adj}(M)\)
adjugate: \([\operatorname{adj}(M)]_{ij}=C_{ji}\) (transpose of the cofactor matrix)


\(\det M\)
determinant of \(M\)


\(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{f}\in\mathbb{R}^n\)
unknown / RHS vectors of the linear system \(M\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{f}\)


\(M^{(i\leftarrow \mathbf{f})}\)
\(M\) with its \(i\)-th column replaced by \(\mathbf{f}\)


\(I\)
\(n\times n\) identity


\(\mathbf{e}_j\)
\(j\)-th standard basis vector of \(\mathbb{R}^n\)


\(\ker M := \{\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n : M\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}\}\)
kernel / null space


\(V_n\)
a linear vector space of dimension \(n\) over \(\mathbb{R}\)

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions

Field is \(\mathbb{R}\). Statements transfer to \(\mathbb{C}\) without modification; finite fields require care (not in scope).
\(n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\), finite.
The cofactor formula \(M^{-1}=\dfrac{1}{\det M}\operatorname{adj}(M)\) was proved in 2025-10-11 morning (construction) and 2025-10-11 afternoon (off-diagonal closure, Theorem 21–22).
The multiplicative law \(\det(AB)=\det(A)\det(B)\) is imported (the teacher flags this explicitly during the session — proof deferred to a later lecture).







NotePrerequisites






Cofactor expansion of \(\det M\) along an arbitrary row/column.
Right-inverse identity \(M^{-1}M=I\) — established in 2025-10-11 afternoon via the cofactor inverse formula \(M^{-1} = \operatorname{adj}(M)/\det M\).
Linear independence of \(n\) vectors in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) via the geometric/volume picture.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Topics covered

Cramer’s rule and its proof from the adjugate formula
Equivalence of left and right inverses for square matrices
The column-upgrade trick: \(A\mathbf{c}_j = \mathbf{0}\) for \(j=1,\dots,n\) with \(\{\mathbf{c}_j\}\) linearly independent \(\;\Rightarrow\;\) \(A = 0\)
Formal definition of a linear vector space (closure under linear combination)
Proof that \(\ker M\) is a linear vector space
The trivial 0-dimensional vector space \(\{\mathbf{0}\}\)

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Theorems

(Cramer’s rule). Let \(M\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) with \(\det M\ne 0\) and \(\mathbf{f}\in\mathbb{R}^n\). The unique solution of \(M\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{f}\) has components
\[
x_i \;=\; \frac{\det M^{(i\leftarrow \mathbf{f})}}{\det M},\qquad i=1,\dots,n. \tag{1}
\]


(Two-sided inverse). If \(M\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) has \(M^{-1}M=I\), then \(MM^{-1}=I\).


(Kernel is a subspace). For any \(M\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), the set \(\ker M\) is a linear subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Derivation of Theorem 1 (Cramer’s rule)
From the adjugate formula \(M^{-1}=\dfrac{1}{\det M}\operatorname{adj}(M)\) (2025-10-11 afternoon, Theorem 22) and \(\mathbf{x}=M^{-1}\mathbf{f}\),
\[
x_i \;=\; \frac{1}{\det M}\sum_{j=1}^{n}[\operatorname{adj}(M)]_{ij}\,f_j \;=\; \frac{1}{\det M}\sum_{j=1}^{n}C_{ji}\,f_j. \tag{2}
\]
The sum \(\sum_j C_{ji}\,f_j\) is, by definition, the cofactor expansion along the \(i\)-th column of the matrix obtained from \(M\) by replacing its \(i\)-th column with \(\mathbf{f}\). That matrix is exactly \(M^{(i\leftarrow \mathbf{f})}\); its column-\(i\) entries are \(f_1,\dots,f_n\) and its cofactors along that column are the \(C_{ji}\) of the original \(M\) (since deleting column \(i\) removes the replaced column entirely, so the remaining submatrix is unchanged). Therefore \(\sum_j C_{ji} f_j = \det M^{(i\leftarrow \mathbf{f})}\), and substituting into (2) yields (1). \(\quad\blacksquare\)



Figure 1. The matrix \(M^{(i\leftarrow \mathbf{f})}\) — column \(i\) replaced by \(\mathbf{f}\), all other columns unchanged.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
section: Derivation A of Theorem 2 — column-upgrade trick (the transcript’s path)
text:
Derivation A of Theorem 2 — column-upgrade trick (the transcript’s path)
Define \(X := MM^{-1}\) (the unknown matrix we want to show equals \(I\)). Multiply \((X-I)\) on the right by \(M\):
\[
(X-I)M \;=\; MM^{-1}M - M \;=\; M\cdot I - M \;=\; 0. \tag{3}
\]
Let \(\mathbf{c}_j\) denote the \(j\)-th column of \(M\). Reading (3) one column at a time:
\[
(X-I)\,\mathbf{c}_j \;=\; \mathbf{0}, \qquad j=1,\dots,n. \tag{4}
\]
From \(M^{-1}M=I\) take determinants: \(\det(M^{-1})\det(M)=1\), so \(\det M\ne 0\). By the geometric criterion (parallelepiped of nonzero volume), the \(n\) columns \(\mathbf{c}_1,\dots,\mathbf{c}_n\) are linearly independent and therefore span \(\mathbb{R}^n\). So every \(\mathbf{v}\in\mathbb{R}^n\) admits a representation \(\mathbf{v}=\sum_j \alpha_j \mathbf{c}_j\), and by linearity of \((X-I)\),
\[
(X-I)\mathbf{v} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_j\,(X-I)\mathbf{c}_j \;=\; \mathbf{0}. \tag{5}
\]
Now test (5) against \(\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{e}_k\) for each \(k=1,\dots,n\). The result \((X-I)\mathbf{e}_k\) is exactly the \(k\)-th column of \(X-I\); all \(n\) columns vanish, so \(X-I=0\), i.e. \(MM^{-1}=I\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)



Figure 2. Two linearly independent columns \(\mathbf{c}_1, \mathbf{c}_2\) of \(M\) generating an arbitrary vector \(\mathbf{v}=\alpha\mathbf{c}_1+\beta\mathbf{c}_2\) via the parallelogram (affine) decomposition.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
section: Derivation B of Theorem 2 — uniqueness of two-sided inverse (independent route)
text:
Derivation B of Theorem 2 — uniqueness of two-sided inverse (independent route)
From \(M^{-1}M=I\) take determinants: \(\det(M^{-1})\det(M)=1\), so \(\det M \ne 0\), hence \(M:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) is injective. By the rank-nullity theorem in finite dimension, an injective linear endomorphism of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is surjective; therefore \(M\) is bijective and admits a unique two-sided inverse \(M^{\dagger}\).
Both \(M^{-1}\) and \(M^{\dagger}\) satisfy the left equation \(XM=I\). Right-multiplying \(M^{-1}M=I\) by \(M^{\dagger}\):
\[
M^{-1}MM^{\dagger} = M^{\dagger} \;\Rightarrow\; M^{-1}\cdot I = M^{\dagger} \;\Rightarrow\; M^{-1}=M^{\dagger}. \tag{6}
\]
Since \(M^{\dagger}\) is two-sided by construction, \(MM^{-1}=MM^{\dagger}=I\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Note on independence. Derivation A imports only “\(\det M \ne 0 \Rightarrow\) columns linearly independent” (geometric). Derivation B imports the more abstract “finite-dim injective endomorphism is surjective” (rank-nullity). The two arguments cite disjoint imported facts; neither uses the other.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
section: Derivation of Theorem 3 (\(\ker M\) is a linear subspace)
text:
Derivation of Theorem 3 (\(\ker M\) is a linear subspace)
Pick \(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in \ker M\) and scalars \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}\). By linearity of matrix–vector multiplication,
\[
M(\alpha\mathbf{x}_1+\beta\mathbf{x}_2) \;=\; \alpha\,M\mathbf{x}_1 + \beta\,M\mathbf{x}_2 \;=\; \alpha\cdot\mathbf{0}+\beta\cdot\mathbf{0} \;=\; \mathbf{0}. \tag{7}
\]
Hence \(\alpha\mathbf{x}_1+\beta\mathbf{x}_2\in\ker M\). The zero vector is in \(\ker M\) trivially (\(M\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}\)). Closure under arbitrary linear combinations plus \(\mathbf{0}\)-containment is exactly the definition of a linear subspace. \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Verification audit


Signs. \(C_{ij}=(-1)^{i+j}\det\widetilde{M}_{ij}\). For \(n=2\), \(M=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\): \(C_{11}=d\), \(C_{12}=-c\), \(C_{21}=-b\), \(C_{22}=a\). Then \(\operatorname{adj}(M)=\begin{pmatrix}C_{11}&C_{21}\\C_{12}&C_{22}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}\), so \(M^{-1}=\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}\) — matches the standard \(2\times 2\) inverse. ✓
Limiting case \(n=1\). \(M=(m)\), \(\det M=m\), Cramer gives \(x_1=f_1/m\), matches direct division. Theorem 2 reduces to “\(m^{-1}\cdot m=1 \Rightarrow m\cdot m^{-1}=1\)” — trivially true in \(\mathbb{R}\). ✓
Special case \(M=I\). \(\operatorname{adj}(I)=I\), \(\det I=1\), \(I^{-1}=I\). Cramer collapses to \(x_i=f_i\). Theorem 2 vacuous. ✓
Degenerate near-miss. If \(\det M=0\), (1) divides by zero — Cramer fails, as required (and \(M^{-1}M=I\) would itself be impossible by determinant multiplicativity, so Thm 2’s hypothesis cannot hold either).
Dependency check. Derivation A uses only the geometric criterion for linear independence and properties of cofactor expansion. Derivation B uses rank-nullity. Neither derivation cites Theorem 2 itself. No circularity. ✓
Hidden assumption flag. Both derivations of Theorem 2 silently rely on \(\det(AB)=\det(A)\det(B)\) to extract \(\det M\ne 0\) from \(M^{-1}M=I\). This identity is imported, not proved in-thread.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Key frames
Four screenshots from the recording, semantically aligned with the derivation above. Each was extracted via ffmpeg -ss <t>, then cropped to remove the participant video panel and downscaled to 1600 px wide.



Frame @ 12m30 — board setup for Theorem 2: \(AB \neq BA\) in general, but for the inverse \(M^{-1}M = I \;\Rightarrow\; MM^{-1} = I\), equivalent to \(M = (M^{-1})^{-1}\).





Frame @ 28m10 — the column-upgrade trick at the board, illustrating eqs. (3)–(5): \((X-I)(\mathbf{c}_1 \mathbf{c}_2 \cdots \mathbf{c}_n) = 0\), then per-column \((X-I)\mathbf{c}_j = 0\) for \(j=1,\dots,n\), then by linearity \((X-I)(\mathbf{c}_1 + \cdots + \mathbf{c}_n) = 0\). The crossed-out \(|X| - |I|\) at the upper-left marks the determinant-only argument that fails to conclude \(X = I\).





Frame @ 41m00 — full board panorama showing the assembled proof: Cramer setup at top, the column-upgrade reasoning in the middle, and Lucas’s parallelogram (affine-coordinates) decomposition \(\alpha\mathbf{c}_1 + \beta\mathbf{c}_2 + \cdots\) at the right.





Frame @ 52m20 — the formal definition of the kernel: \(V = \{\,\mathbf{x} \mid M\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}\,\} = \ker M\), alongside the singular-matrix condition \(|M| = 0\) that makes \(\ker M\) nontrivial. This is the setup for Theorem 3.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Dependency map of this lesson





flowchart TB
    A["Cofactor formula<br/>M⁻¹ = adj(M)/det(M)<br/><i>(imported)</i>"] --> B["Cramer's rule<br/>eq. (1)–(2)<br/>Theorem 1"]
    A --> C["M⁻¹M = I<br/><i>(imported)</i>"]
    C --> D["(X − I)M = 0<br/>eq. (3)"]
    D --> E["(X − I)c_j = 0 ∀j<br/>eq. (4)"]
    F["det M ≠ 0 ⇒<br/>columns linearly independent<br/><i>(imported geometric)</i>"] --> G["Span argument<br/>eq. (5)"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["X − I = 0<br/>MM⁻¹ = I<br/>Theorem 2 (Derivation A)"]
    C --> I["det(M)·det(M⁻¹)=1<br/><i>via det(AB)=det A · det B</i>"]
    I --> J["M is bijective<br/>(rank-nullity)<br/><i>(imported)</i>"]
    J --> K["Unique two-sided inverse<br/>eq. (6)<br/>Theorem 2 (Derivation B)"]
    L["Linearity of matrix multiplication"] --> M["M(αx₁+βx₂)=αMx₁+βMx₂<br/>eq. (7)"]
    M --> N["ker M closed under<br/>linear combinations<br/>Theorem 3"]

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Elaine: “Can we say the original matrix is the inverse of the inverse matrix?” Teacher: Not yet — that’s the conclusion we’re after, not what we already have. The construction so far only warrants \(M^{-1}M=I\).
Teaching move: sharply separating what is to be proved from what is known. The conclusion \(M=(M^{-1})^{-1}\) is logically equivalent to \(MM^{-1}=I\), but invoking it as a step would be circular at this point.


Toby: (proof by contradiction) “Suppose the \((i,j)\) entry of \(X-I\) is nonzero. Take \(\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{e}_j\). Then \((X-I)\mathbf{v}\) has the nonzero entry in position \(i\) — contradiction.” Lucas: (direct basis test) “Take \(\mathbf{v}\) to be each \(\mathbf{e}_k\) in turn — that gives you every column of \(X-I\) as zero.”
Teaching move: two valid ways to upgrade “(X−I)v=0 for all v” to “X−I=0.” Lucas’s direct version generalises cleanly; Toby’s contradiction version captures the same content but with one extra rhetorical step. The course will reuse Lucas’s pattern repeatedly.

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework. Prove that the range of \(M\), \[
\operatorname{range}(M) := \{\,M\mathbf{u} : \mathbf{u}\in\mathbb{R}^n\,\},
\] is a linear subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^n\).
Hint: mimic the closure test used for Theorem 3 — take \(\mathbf{w}_1=M\mathbf{u}_1\), \(\mathbf{w}_2=M\mathbf{u}_2\), and exhibit a single \(\mathbf{u}\) such that \(M\mathbf{u}=\alpha\mathbf{w}_1+\beta\mathbf{w}_2\).

title: Cramer’s Rule, Two-Sided Inverse, and Linear Vector Spaces
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step. Derivation A’s invocation of “\(\det M\ne 0 \Rightarrow\) columns linearly independent” via the parallelepiped/volume picture. Geometrically compelling for \(n\le 3\); for general \(n\) the rigorous proof goes through the Laplace expansion and the multiplicative law \(\det(AB)=\det A\cdot\det B\) — neither derived in-thread.
Implicit assumptions. Field is \(\mathbb{R}\) (or characteristic 0); \(n\) finite. Extension to commutative rings is possible but out of scope.
Imported facts that should be revisited. (i) The adjugate formula for \(M^{-1}\); (ii) \(\det(AB)=\det A\cdot\det B\). Both are scheduled for the “determinants from scratch” lecture.
Confidence. Theorem statements: high. Proofs as presented: medium-high — load-bearing identities are imported rather than derived. The lesson would survive recasting in a fully self-contained development, with cost equal to the imported lemmas above.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Lead
Promotes the matrix eigenvalue equation \(M \cdot E = E \cdot \Lambda\) (Feb 21 homework) to its per-vector form \((M - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\). The non-trivial-solution requirement gives \(\det(M - \lambda I) = 0\) — the characteristic polynomial. The session then proves that this polynomial has degree exactly \(n\) in \(\lambda\), leading coefficient \((-1)^n\), and (by Vieta) recovers the trace and determinant relations \(\sum \lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}(M)\), \(\prod \lambda_i = \det(M)\) — without assuming the matrix is symmetric or even diagonalisable. The constant-term-via-\(\lambda=0\) trick gives the determinant identity in one line.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Symbol dictionary








Symbol
Meaning




\(M \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) (or \(\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\))
general square matrix


\(\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n\)
candidate eigenvector


\(\lambda \in \mathbb{C}\)
candidate eigenvalue (complex in general)


\(I\)
\(n\times n\) identity


\(M - \lambda I\)
“shifted” matrix; diagonal entries \(m_{ii} - \lambda\), off-diagonal entries unchanged


\(p(\lambda) := \det(M - \lambda I)\)
characteristic polynomial of \(M\)


\(\operatorname{tr}(M) = \sum_i m_{ii}\)
trace


\(\det(M) = p(0) \cdot (-1)^n\)
determinant (via characteristic polynomial evaluated at \(\lambda = 0\))


“Fixed subspace”
a 1-dim subspace \(\operatorname{span}(\mathbf{v})\) such that \(M(\operatorname{span}(\mathbf{v})) \subseteq \operatorname{span}(\mathbf{v})\)

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions

Cramer’s rule classification (2025-10-11 afternoon, Theorem 24): a linear system \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}\) has non-trivial solutions iff \(\det A = 0\).
Cofactor / Laplace expansion of \(\det A\) along any row or column.
Vieta’s formulas for a degree-\(n\) polynomial \(a_n\lambda^n + a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0\): sum of roots \(= -a_{n-1}/a_n\), product of roots \(= (-1)^n a_0/a_n\).
Determinant geometry (parallelepiped volume) — same as in 2025-10-11-morning.
Imported facts: existence of \(n\) complex eigenvalues counted with multiplicity (fundamental theorem of algebra applied to the characteristic polynomial).

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Topics covered

Linear-transformation viewpoint of \(M\) acting on vectors \(\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n\)
“Fixed subspace” interpretation of eigenvectors (preserved direction, scaled by \(\lambda\))
Subtracting a scalar from a matrix: requires lifting \(\lambda \to \lambda I\) first
\((M - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\) as the per-vector eigenvalue equation
\(\det(M - \lambda I) = 0\) via Cramer’s-rule non-trivial-solution criterion
Degree analysis of the characteristic polynomial: leading term \((-\lambda)^n\)
The \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient: requires using \(n-1\) diagonals, picking the constant on the remaining row
Trace recovery: \(\sum \lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}(M)\)
Determinant recovery: evaluate \(p(\lambda)\) at \(\lambda = 0\) to extract the constant term in one line

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Derivation of Theorem 42 (per-vector eigenvalue equation)
Starting from \(M\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}\) and bringing \(\lambda\mathbf{v}\) to the LHS: \(M\mathbf{v} - \lambda\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\). Cannot factor as “\((M - \lambda)\mathbf{v}\)” — \(M\) is a matrix, \(\lambda\) is a scalar; not the same kind of object. The fix: promote \(\lambda\) to \(\lambda I\) (the scalar matrix with \(\lambda\) on the diagonal): \[
\lambda\mathbf{v} \;=\; (\lambda I)\,\mathbf{v}.
\] Now \(M\) and \(\lambda I\) are both \(n \times n\) matrices, so \[
M\mathbf{v} - (\lambda I)\,\mathbf{v} \;=\; (M - \lambda I)\,\mathbf{v} \;=\; \mathbf{0}. \tag{45}
\] With \(\mathbf{v} \ne \mathbf{0}\) as the eigenvector requirement. \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Derivation of Theorem 43 (characteristic polynomial)
The equation \((M - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\) is a homogeneous linear system in \(\mathbf{v}\). By Cramer’s-rule classification (Thm 24), it has non-trivial solutions iff \(\det(M - \lambda I) = 0\).
Why degree exactly \(n\), leading coefficient \((-1)^n\): Write \(M - \lambda I\) with diagonal \((m_{ii} - \lambda)\) and off-diagonal \(m_{ij}\) unchanged. The determinant picks one entry from each row and column. The unique combination of \(n\) entries all carrying a \(\lambda\) is the diagonal product: \[
(m_{11} - \lambda)(m_{22} - \lambda)\cdots(m_{nn} - \lambda) \;=\; (-\lambda)^n + (\text{lower in }\lambda).
\] All other determinant terms involve at least one off-diagonal \(m_{ij}\) entry, which is \(\lambda\)-free, giving lower degrees. So \(p(\lambda) = (-1)^n \lambda^n + \cdots\), degree exactly \(n\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
section: Derivation of Theorem 44a (trace recovery — Vieta on \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient)
text:
Derivation of Theorem 44a (trace recovery — Vieta on \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient)
Expand the diagonal product: \[
\prod_{i=1}^{n}(m_{ii} - \lambda) \;=\; (-\lambda)^n + (-\lambda)^{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{ii} + \cdots
\] The \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient is \((-1)^{n-1}\operatorname{tr}(M)\).
Off-diagonal terms cannot contribute to \(\lambda^{n-1}\): any term using an off-diagonal \(m_{ij}\) entry (with \(i \ne j\)) eliminates two diagonals at once (row \(i\) AND column \(j\) each lose their diagonal entry). So such terms have at most \(n - 2\) \(\lambda\)-bearing factors, contributing only \(\lambda^{n-2}\) or lower.
By Vieta on \(p(\lambda) = (-1)^n \lambda^n + (-1)^{n-1}\operatorname{tr}(M)\,\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots\): \[
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i \;=\; -\frac{(-1)^{n-1}\operatorname{tr}(M)}{(-1)^n} \;=\; \operatorname{tr}(M). \qquad\blacksquare
\]

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
section: Derivation of Theorem 44b (determinant recovery — the \(\lambda = 0\) trick)
text:
Derivation of Theorem 44b (determinant recovery — the \(\lambda = 0\) trick)
Constant term: \(p(0) = \det(M - 0 \cdot I) = \det(M)\). By Vieta: \[
\prod_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i \;=\; (-1)^n \frac{p(0)}{(-1)^n} \;=\; \det(M). \qquad\blacksquare
\]
Dramatically shorter than the trace argument — entire determinant identity in one substitution.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
section: Derivation B of Theorem 44 (independent — similarity invariance)
text:
Derivation B of Theorem 44 (independent — similarity invariance)
If \(M\) is diagonalisable, \(M = E \Lambda E^{-1}\) with \(\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)\). Then by trace cyclicity (\(\operatorname{tr}(ABC) = \operatorname{tr}(BCA)\)) and multiplicative determinant law: \[
\operatorname{tr}(M) = \operatorname{tr}(\Lambda) = \sum \lambda_i, \qquad
\det(M) = \det(\Lambda) = \prod \lambda_i.
\] Note: this derivation imports two deeper facts and only works for diagonalisable matrices. Derivation A works for all matrices and uses lighter imports.
Note on independence. Derivation A: cofactor expansion + Vieta. Derivation B: trace cyclicity + det multiplicativity. Disjoint imports.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Verification audit


\(n = 1\). \(M = (m)\), \(p(\lambda) = m - \lambda\), root \(\lambda_1 = m\). Trace = det = \(m = \lambda_1\). ✓
\(n = 2\). \(M = \begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\), \(p(\lambda) = (a-\lambda)(d-\lambda) - bc = \lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + (ad-bc)\). Vieta: \(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = a+d = \operatorname{tr}\) ✓, \(\lambda_1\lambda_2 = ad-bc = \det\) ✓. Recovers Theorem 38 from 2026-02-21 morning.
Worked \(3 \times 3\). \(M = \begin{pmatrix}2&1&0\\0&3&0\\1&0&4\end{pmatrix}\), block lower-triangular. Eigenvalues \(\{2, 3, 4\}\). Trace: \(2+3+4=9\) ✓. Determinant: \(2\cdot 3\cdot 4 = 24\) ✓.
Complex eigenvalues OK. \(M = \begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) (90° rotation). \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^2 + 1\), roots \(\pm i\). Trace \(= 0 = i+(-i)\) ✓, det \(= 1 = i\cdot(-i)\) ✓.
Defective (non-diagonalisable) matrix. \(M = \begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\). \(p(\lambda) = (2-\lambda)^2\), double root \(\lambda = 2\). Vieta: \(2+2 = 4 = \operatorname{tr}\) ✓, \(2\cdot 2 = 4 = \det\) ✓. Theorem 44 holds without diagonalisability.
Degree sanity. \(n = 3\): \(p(\lambda)\) has degree 3, leading coefficient \((-1)^3 = -1\). ✓
Dependency check. Theorem 42: algebra. Theorem 43: Cramer-classification + cofactor structure. Theorem 44 (A): polynomial-degree analysis + Vieta + \(\lambda=0\) trick. Theorem 44 (B): trace cyclicity + det multiplicativity. No circularity. ✓

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Lecture video

  
  Your browser does not support HTML5 video.


  Individual session (Catherine + Elaine) · hosted on
  GitHub Release v0.4
  · also viewable in Google Drive

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Key frames



Frame @ 05m00 — Recap of conic-diagonalization; linear-transformation view of \(M\) acting on \(\mathbf{v}\).





Frame @ 22m00 — “Fixed subspace” picture: \(M\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}\) as preserved direction, scaled by \(\lambda\). Bundling into matrix form \(ME = E\Lambda\).





Frame @ 38m00 — The \(\lambda \to \lambda I\) lift resolving the scalar-matrix-subtraction type mismatch. Result: \((M - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\).





Frame @ 52m00 — Degree analysis of \(\det(M - \lambda I)\) as \(n\)-degree polynomial; diagonal product gives \((-\lambda)^n\); Vieta + \(\lambda=0\) trick recover trace and determinant.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Dependency map





flowchart TB
    A["M·v = λ·v<br/>per-vector eigen<br/><i>(Feb 21 homework)</i>"] --> B["Bring λv to LHS:<br/>M·v − λv = 0"]
    B --> C["Type mismatch:<br/>M matrix vs λ scalar"]
    C --> D["Lift λ → λI<br/>now both n×n"]
    D --> E["(M − λI)·v = 0<br/>Theorem 42"]
    F["Cramer classification:<br/>nontrivial soln iff det = 0<br/><i>(Oct 11 afternoon)</i>"] --> G["det(M − λI) = 0<br/>characteristic poly<br/>Theorem 43"]
    E --> G
    H["Diagonal product Π(m_ii − λ)<br/>gives (−λ)ⁿ leading"] --> I["p(λ) degree n,<br/>leading coef (−1)ⁿ"]
    I --> J["[λ^(n−1)] coef =<br/>(−1)^(n−1)·tr(M)"]
    K["Off-diag terms<br/>knock out 2 diagonals each<br/>⇒ only ≤ λ^(n−2)"] --> J
    J --> L["Vieta: Σλᵢ = tr(M)<br/>Theorem 44a"]
    M["p(0) = det(M − 0·I) = det(M)"] --> N["Vieta: Πλᵢ = det(M)<br/>Theorem 44b<br/><b>'λ=0 trick'</b>"]
    L -.matches.-> P["Feb 21 morning result<br/>for n=2 (Theorem 38)"]
    N -.matches.-> P

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Teacher: “From \(M\mathbf{v} - \lambda\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\), can I pull out \(\mathbf{v}\) and write \((M - \lambda)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\)?” Catherine: (hesitates) Teacher: “If I gave you \(\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\) and asked you to subtract 7, what would you do? Five dogs minus seven dogs is fine. But five dogs minus one cat — what’s left?” Catherine: “Oh — different types.” Teacher: “Right. Lift the cat to a herd: \(\lambda \to \lambda I\). Now they’re the same animal.”
Teaching move: type-system intuition — algebraic operations require like-typed objects. Scalar–matrix mismatch is a real bug, silently fixed by inserting an identity. Making the lift explicit clarifies what was previously hand-waved.


Teacher: “What’s the constant term of this hugely complicated \(n\)-degree polynomial?” Catherine: “Too complicated to find by expansion?” Teacher: “Plug in \(\lambda = 0\).” Catherine: “Oh — and that’s just \(\det(M)\)!” Teacher: “Brilliant. The whole determinant identity in one line.”
Teaching move: substitution as evaluation shortcut — when only the value at a specific input is needed, substitute instead of expanding symbolically.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework.

Verify the \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient extraction for \(n = 3\) by explicit expansion. Confirm against the trace prediction.
Compute eigenvalues of \(M = \begin{pmatrix}3&-2\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) via \(\det(M - \lambda I) = 0\). Then find eigenvectors by solving \((M - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\) for each \(\lambda_i\).
Preview for next session: what happens when the characteristic polynomial has a repeated root? Sketch the eigenvector picture.

title: Eigenvalue Equation det(M−λI) = 0 Derived End-to-End; Trace and Determinant from the Characteristic Polynomial
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step. The \(\lambda^{n-1}\) coefficient argument relies on the combinatorial claim “off-diagonal terms knock out two diagonals each.” This is correct but the lesson sketches rather than fully formalises it. The formal proof: any non-identity permutation \(\sigma \in S_n\) contributing to \(\det(M-\lambda I)\) has at least 2 indices \(i\) with \(\sigma(i) \ne i\), so its contributing term has at most \(n - 2\) factors of \((m_{ii} - \lambda)\).
Imported facts. Cramer’s-rule classification (proved 2025-10-11 afternoon); cofactor expansion; Vieta’s formulas; fundamental theorem of algebra. Multiplicative determinant law used only in Derivation B.
Diagonalisability silently varies. Theorem 44 holds for all matrices (Vieta works on roots-with-multiplicity), but the lesson doesn’t explicitly distinguish “\(n\) eigenvalues” from “\(n\) linearly independent eigenvectors.” The Jordan-block case (defective matrices) gets formal treatment in 2026-04-04 morning.
Real vs complex eigenvalues. Symmetric matrices guarantee real eigenvalues; general matrices may have complex ones (e.g., the 90° rotation example). All theorems hold over \(\mathbb{C}\).
Confidence. Theorem 42, 43 + proofs: high. Theorem 44 Derivation A: high (the trace argument needs the combinatorial gloss noted above). Derivation B: high but limited to diagonalisable matrices.

Sequels:

2026-03-28 morning (stub) — Gram-matrix rank tests; pivots to Gauss-Jordan inversion.
2026-04-04 morning (stub) — companion matrix + Jordan-block treatment of repeated eigenvalues (homework #3 above). Defective case formal treatment.
2026-05-02 morning (stub) — Cayley–Hamilton \(p(M) = 0\) strengthens Theorem 43.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text: Extends the \(\tan(2\theta) = 2b/(a-c)\) rotation-angle formula — derived in the hyperbolic case via real asymptote slopes acting as angle bisectors — to the elliptical case where no real asymptotes exist. Technique: substitute \(y \to yi\), converting the elliptic equation into a hyperbolic one with complex slopes; the angle-bisector identity still holds algebraically; the same formula gives the real rotation angle. Confirms (68) is type-independent.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text: Extends the \(\tan(2\theta) = 2b/(a-c)\) rotation-angle formula — derived in the hyperbolic case via real asymptote slopes acting as angle bisectors — to the elliptical case where no real asymptotes exist. Technique: substitute \(y \to yi\), converting the elliptic equation into a hyperbolic one with complex slopes; the angle-bisector identity still holds algebraically; the same formula gives the real rotation angle. Confirms (68) is type-independent.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Symbol dictionary








Symbol
Meaning




\(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\)
quadratic form of centred conic


\(\Delta = b^2 - ac\)
discriminant (\(>0\) hyperbola, \(<0\) ellipse, \(=0\) parabola)


\(k_1, k_2\)
slopes of factor lines (\(ck^2 + 2bk + a = 0\)); real for hyperbola, complex conjugate for ellipse


\(\theta\)
rotation angle to symmetric axes

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Primitive notions

Hyperbolic factorisation: \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = c(y - k_1 x)(y - k_2 x)\) when \(\Delta > 0\).
Angle bisector: symmetric axis at angle \(\theta\) with \(2\theta = \arctan k_1 + \arctan k_2\).
Tangent addition: \(\tan(\alpha + \beta) = \frac{\tan\alpha + \tan\beta}{1 - \tan\alpha\tan\beta}\).
Vieta on \(ck^2 + 2bk + a = 0\): \(k_1 + k_2 = -2b/c\), \(k_1 k_2 = a/c\).

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Theorem

(Universal rotation-angle formula). For any centred conic \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = \text{const}\), \[
\tan(2\theta) \;=\; \frac{2b}{a - c}. \tag{68}
\]

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Derivation A — hyperbolic case (recap)
Real asymptote slopes \(k_1, k_2\). Tangent of bisector angle: \[
\tan(2\theta) = \frac{k_1 + k_2}{1 - k_1 k_2} = \frac{-2b/c}{1 - a/c} = \frac{-2b/c}{(c-a)/c} = \frac{2b}{a-c}. \quad\blacksquare
\]

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
section: Derivation B — elliptic case via \(y \to yi\) substitution
text:
Derivation B — elliptic case via \(y \to yi\) substitution
For ellipse (\(\Delta < 0\)), \(ck^2 + 2bk + a = 0\) has complex-conjugate roots \(k_1 = p + qi, k_2 = p - qi\).
Substitution. \(y \to yi\) turns \(cy^2 = c(yi)^2 = -cy^2\). The transformed equation is hyperbolic in \((x, y)\), so it factors with two complex linear factors. The slopes \(\kappa_{1,2}\) satisfy a quadratic with real coefficients and complex-conjugate roots.
Key insight. The formula \(\tan(2\theta) = (k_1 + k_2)/(1 - k_1 k_2)\) depends only on Vieta sums and products, which are real for complex-conjugate roots: - \(k_1 + k_2 = 2p\) (real) - \(k_1 k_2 = p^2 + q^2\) (real)
The same Vieta values come from the original \(ck^2 + 2bk + a = 0\): \(-2b/c\) and \(a/c\). So: \[
\tan(2\theta) = \frac{-2b/c}{1 - a/c} = \frac{2b}{a-c}. \quad\blacksquare
\]

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Verification audit


Ellipse \(4x^2 + 2xy + y^2 = 4\). \(\Delta = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0\). Formula: \(\tan(2\theta) = 2/(4-1) = 2/3\). \(2\theta \approx 33.7°\), \(\theta \approx 16.85°\). ✓
Vieta sanity. \(k^2 + 2k + 4 = 0\) ⇒ \(k_{1,2} = -1 \pm i\sqrt{3}\). Sum \(= -2 = -2b/c\) ✓. Product \(= 4 = a/c\) ✓. \(\tan(2\theta) = -2/(1-4) = 2/3\) ✓.
Hyperbola \(x^2 - y^2 = c\). \(a=1, b=0, c=-1\). \(\tan(2\theta) = 0/2 = 0\) ⇒ \(\theta = 0\). ✓ (axes already aligned)
Already-rotated. \(b = 0\) ⇒ \(\tan(2\theta) = 0\) ⇒ no rotation needed. ✓
Symmetry \(a \leftrightarrow c\). Flips sign of \(\tan(2\theta)\) — mirror reflection. ✓
Dependency. A: real Vieta + tangent addition. B: same identities, complex roots that conjugate-pair to real. No circularity.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Teacher: “No real asymptotes for ellipses — geometric proof breaks. How proceed?” Students: “Use \(y \to yi\) to convert into algebraic hyperbola.” Teacher: “Slopes become complex, conjugate pairs, sum/product real. Formula survives.”
Teaching move: complex detour preserves formula — generic technique when real-case geometric argument fails but algebra still works.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework. Solve \(4x^2 + 2xy + y^2 = 4\): apply (68); compute \(\tan\theta\) via \(2t/(1-t^2) = 2/3\); get \(\cos\theta, \sin\theta\) via \(1+t^2 = \sec^2\theta\); substitute \(u = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta\), \(v = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta\); verify diagonalization on Desmos.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step. Derivation B’s Vieta-reality relies on real coefficients \((a, b, c)\). Complex coefficients would break.
Imported facts. Vieta; tangent addition; hyperbolic factorisation.
Geometric meaning loss in elliptic case. Complex slopes don’t draw; symmetric axes equivalently = major/minor axes of bounding box.
Confidence. High. Two derivations of same formula confirm.

title: Symmetric-Axis Formula for Elliptical Conics via the y → yi Substitution
text:
Related sessions

Companion morning: 2026-02-21 morning derives \(\tan(2\theta) = 2b/(a-c)\) via trace/det invariance — matrix approach. This afternoon: algebraic \(y \to yi\) detour. Two proofs of same identity.
Precursors: 2025-10-04, 2025-10-18 morning.
Forward: complex eigenvalues of rotation matrices (Apr 4 afternoon) — same complex-coordinate technique.

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text: Reformulates Pascal’s-triangle binomial coefficients as lattice-walk counts on an \(n \times n\) grid: the number of monotone paths from one corner to the diagonally opposite corner is \(\binom{2n}{n}\). The session sets up Catalan numbers as a restricted count (walks constrained never to cross the diagonal) and introduces generating functions as algebraic containers for combinatorial sequences — paving the way for the generating-function machinery used in later sessions (2026-01-24 morning and 2026-04-04 morning).

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text: Reformulates Pascal’s-triangle binomial coefficients as lattice-walk counts on an \(n \times n\) grid: the number of monotone paths from one corner to the diagonally opposite corner is \(\binom{2n}{n}\). The session sets up Catalan numbers as a restricted count (walks constrained never to cross the diagonal) and introduces generating functions as algebraic containers for combinatorial sequences — paving the way for the generating-function machinery used in later sessions (2026-01-24 morning and 2026-04-04 morning).

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Symbol dictionary




Symbol
Meaning




\(\binom{n}{k}\)
binomial coefficient


\(C_n\)
\(n\)-th Catalan number


board\(_{ij}\)
lattice-path count from origin to \((i,j)\)


\(G(x) = \sum a_n x^n\)
generating function

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Theorems

(Lattice-walk count). Monotone (right/up only) paths from \((0,0)\) to \((n,n)\): \[
\binom{2n}{n}. \tag{69}
\]


(Pascal recursion via lattice). \(\binom{i+j}{i} = \binom{i+j-1}{i-1} + \binom{i+j-1}{i}\). \(\tag{70}\)


(Pascal-triangle preservation under truncation). Restricting to box \([0,n]^2\) preserves Pascal values up to the diagonal; below-diagonal values are reduced due to lost boundary contributions.

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Derivation of Theorem 69
A monotone path is an ordering of \(n\) right-steps and \(n\) up-steps. Number of orderings = \(\binom{2n}{n}\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Derivation of Theorem 70
Last step into \((i,j)\) is either from \((i-1,j)\) or \((i,j-1)\) (disjoint, exhaustive). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Derivation of Theorem 71
Diagonal entries depend only on inputs from upper-left triangle (within the box), so they match Pascal. Below-diagonal entries would need outside-box contributions, hence reduced. \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Lattice Walks, Pascal’s Triangle, and Generating Functions: Setting Up Catalan Numbers
text:
Verification audit


\(n=1\). \(\binom{2}{1} = 2\): RU, UR. ✓
\(n=2\). \(\binom{4}{2} = 6\): RRUU, RURU, RUUR, URRU, URUR, UURR. ✓
Pascal at \((1,1)\). \(\binom{2}{1} = 2 = 1+1\). ✓
Diagonal preservation. \((3,3)\) in \(3\times 3\) box: \(\binom{6}{3} = 20\). ✓
Dependency. Counting + case analysis + induction. No circularity.

Weakest step. Truncation argument needs careful induction; sketch in transcript.
Catalan setup only. Full \(C(x) = (1-\sqrt{1-4x})/(2x)\) deferred to later sessions.
Meta content. ~30% methodological (BQ, Brayden intro, AI discussion).
Confidence. High.

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text: An AMC 2015 #25 tangent-circle problem opens the chapter on infinite series. Two circles tangent to a common line, with radii \(R\) and \(r\), have tangency-point separation \(s = 2\sqrt{Rr}\). The lesson uses a self-similar generating-sum argument to obtain the geometric series closed form, then (Lucas’s observation) factors the geometric series via binary representation: \(1 + x + x^2 + \cdots = \prod_{k=0}^{\infty}(1 + x^{2^k})\). The product unfolds because every non-negative integer has a unique binary representation.

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text: An AMC 2015 #25 tangent-circle problem opens the chapter on infinite series. Two circles tangent to a common line, with radii \(R\) and \(r\), have tangency-point separation \(s = 2\sqrt{Rr}\). The lesson uses a self-similar generating-sum argument to obtain the geometric series closed form, then (Lucas’s observation) factors the geometric series via binary representation: \(1 + x + x^2 + \cdots = \prod_{k=0}^{\infty}(1 + x^{2^k})\). The product unfolds because every non-negative integer has a unique binary representation.

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Theorems

(Tangent-circle separation). Two circles tangent to a common line and externally tangent to each other, with radii \(R, r\): tangency-point separation \(s = 2\sqrt{Rr}\). \(\tag{75}\)


(Geometric series, closed form). For \(|x| < 1\): \[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n = \frac{1}{1-x}. \tag{76}
\]


(Binary factorization of geometric series). For \(|x| < 1\): \[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n = \prod_{k=0}^{\infty} (1 + x^{2^k}) \;=\; (1+x)(1+x^2)(1+x^4)(1+x^8)\cdots \tag{77}
\]

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Derivation of Theorem 75
Place the common tangent line as \(y = 0\) with circles’ centers at \((x_1, R)\) and \((x_2, r)\). Centers’ distance equals \(R + r\) (external tangency). So \(\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (r - R)^2} = R + r\). Squaring: \((x_2 - x_1)^2 = (R+r)^2 - (R-r)^2 = 4Rr\), giving \(|x_2 - x_1| = 2\sqrt{Rr}\) — which is the tangency-point separation along the line. \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Derivation of Theorem 76
Let \(S = 1 + x + x^2 + \cdots\). Then \(xS = x + x^2 + x^3 + \cdots = S - 1\), so \(S(1 - x) = 1\), giving \(S = 1/(1-x)\). (Convergence requires \(|x| < 1\).) \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Derivation of Theorem 77 (binary unfolding)
Expand the product \(\prod_{k=0}^{\infty}(1 + x^{2^k})\) term-by-term: each factor contributes either 1 or \(x^{2^k}\) when multiplied out. So the product equals \[
\sum_{S \subseteq \{0,1,2,\dots\}\text{ finite}} x^{\sum_{k \in S} 2^k}.
\] The exponents range over \(\sum_{k \in S} 2^k\) — but every non-negative integer \(n\) has a unique binary representation \(n = \sum_{k \in S_n} 2^k\). So the exponents cover each non-negative integer exactly once, and the product equals \(\sum_{n \ge 0} x^n\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Verification audit


Tangent circles \(R=4, r=1\). \(s = 2\sqrt{4\cdot 1} = 4\). ✓
Geometric at \(x = 1/2\). \(S = 1/(1-1/2) = 2\). Sum \(1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + \cdots = 2\). ✓
Binary factorization at \(x = 1/2\). \((1+1/2)(1+1/4)(1+1/16)\cdots = (3/2)(5/4)(17/16)(257/256)\cdots\). Numerically: \(1.5 \cdot 1.25 = 1.875\); \(\cdot 1.0625 = 1.9921875\); \(\cdot 1.00390625 \approx 1.9999...\); converges to 2. ✓
Truncated check at \(x = 1/3\). \((1+1/3)(1+1/9)(1+1/81) = (4/3)(10/9)(82/81) = 3280/2187 \approx 1.500\); geometric: \(1/(1-1/3) = 1.5\). ✓
Dependency. Thm 75: Pythagorean. Thm 76: self-similar shift. Thm 77: unique binary representation. No circularity.

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Fragility summary


Convergence requires \(|x| < 1\) for both series and product to make sense.
Binary uniqueness is the bottom-line import; deep result of number theory (Stern-Brocot tree, base representation theory) but accepted as elementary.
Confidence. All three theorems: high.

title: Tangent-Circle Chain: Self-Similar Infinite Series and the Binary Factorization 1 + x + x² + … = ∏(1 + x(2k))
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework. Determine whether \(\binom{211}{143}\) is even or odd using Kummer’s theorem (count carries in binary addition of \(143 + 68 = 211\)).

title: Asymptotic Analysis: When to Drop Infinitesimal Terms
text: Reviews horizontal vs slant asymptotes for rational functions, then articulates the general principle distinguishing when infinitesimal terms can be discarded (limit is a number) from when they cannot (limit is a function — slant asymptote requires keeping more terms). Teacher’s repeated frustration with students mechanically applying polynomial-specific rules instead of the general “what counts as small enough” philosophy.

title: Asymptotic Analysis: When to Drop Infinitesimal Terms
text: Reviews horizontal vs slant asymptotes for rational functions, then articulates the general principle distinguishing when infinitesimal terms can be discarded (limit is a number) from when they cannot (limit is a function — slant asymptote requires keeping more terms). Teacher’s repeated frustration with students mechanically applying polynomial-specific rules instead of the general “what counts as small enough” philosophy.

title: Asymptotic Analysis: When to Drop Infinitesimal Terms
text:
Theorems

(Horizontal asymptote). For \(R(x) = p(x)/q(x)\) with \(\deg p = \deg q\): \[
\lim_{|x|\to\infty} R(x) = \frac{\text{leading coef of }p}{\text{leading coef of }q}. \tag{79}
\] If \(\deg p < \deg q\), the limit is \(0\).


(Slant asymptote — keep more terms). For \(\deg p = \deg q + 1\), long-divide \(p \div q\) to get \(R(x) = Q(x) + r(x)/q(x)\) with \(\deg Q = 1\). The slant asymptote is \(y = Q(x)\). Cannot simply drop sub-leading numerator terms (as one would for a horizontal-asymptote computation) — the linear coefficient of \(Q\) depends on them. \(\tag{80}\)


(General infinitesimal principle). When computing \(\lim_{x \to a} f(x)\):

If the limit is a number, drop terms that go to zero in the limit.
If the limit is a function of \(x\) (asymptote), drop only terms that go to zero uniformly relative to the asymptote’s leading order.

The distinction: \(1/x \to 0\) as \(x \to \infty\), but \(x \cdot (1/x) = 1 \ne 0\), so \(1/x\) cannot be dropped if multiplied by a growing factor.

title: Asymptotic Analysis: When to Drop Infinitesimal Terms
text:
Verification audit


Horizontal asymptote \(R(x) = (2x^2 + 3x + 1)/(x^2 - 5)\). Leading coefficients \(2/1 = 2\). ✓
Slant \(R(x) = (x^2 + 3x + 1)/(x - 1)\). Long-divide: \(R = x + 4 + 5/(x-1)\). Slant \(y = x + 4\). Dropping \(3x\) from numerator before long division would give \(y = x + 0\), wrong by 4. ✓ Demonstrates why sub-leading terms matter for slant asymptotes.
General principle worked example. \(\lim_{x\to\infty} (x^2 + x)/(x + 1) = ?\) Long-divide: \(= x + 0 + 0/(x+1) = x\). The slant asymptote is \(y = x\) (function, not number). Dropping the “\(+x\)” from numerator before division would give \(\lim x^2/(x+1) = x\) — coincidentally correct here, but pure luck. The principled approach is to long-divide.

title: Asymptotic Analysis: When to Drop Infinitesimal Terms
text:
Fragility summary


Heuristic limit. “Drop terms that go to zero” only works when the answer is a number. For asymptotes the answer is a function, and uniformity of vanishing matters.
Long division is the safe path. Always works; the heuristic shortcuts are tempting but error-prone.
Confidence. All three theorems: high (calculus-level standard).

title: Polynomial Snaking Method; Rational Functions and Slant Asymptotes via Long Division
text: Introduces the snaking method for graphing polynomials with multiple real roots (alternating signs in intervals between roots), then extends to rational functions via polynomial long division to extract slant asymptotes and analyze approach direction from the remainder’s leading term.

title: Polynomial Snaking Method; Rational Functions and Slant Asymptotes via Long Division
text: Introduces the snaking method for graphing polynomials with multiple real roots (alternating signs in intervals between roots), then extends to rational functions via polynomial long division to extract slant asymptotes and analyze approach direction from the remainder’s leading term.

title: Polynomial Snaking Method; Rational Functions and Slant Asymptotes via Long Division
text:
Theorems

(Polynomial snaking). For a polynomial \(p(x)\) with distinct real roots \(r_1 < r_2 < \cdots < r_k\) (with multiplicities), the sign of \(p(x)\) alternates across each odd-multiplicity root and stays the same across each even-multiplicity root. The graph crosses the \(x\)-axis at odd-multiplicity roots and bounces (tangent) at even-multiplicity roots. \(\tag{83}\)


(Slant asymptote via long division). For \(R(x) = p(x)/q(x)\) with \(\deg p = \deg q + 1\), long division yields \(R(x) = Q(x) + r(x)/q(x)\) where \(Q\) is linear and \(r(x)/q(x) \to 0\) as \(|x| \to \infty\). The slant asymptote is \(y = Q(x)\); the sign of \(r(x)\) for large \(|x|\) tells whether the graph approaches the asymptote from above or below. \(\tag{84}\)

title: Polynomial Snaking Method; Rational Functions and Slant Asymptotes via Long Division
text:
Verification audit


Snaking on \(p(x) = x(x-2)^2(x+1)\). Roots \(-1, 0, 2\) with multiplicities \(1, 1, 2\). As \(x \to \infty\): \(p > 0\). Crossing at \(x = 2\) → no (even multiplicity, bounces). Crossing at \(x = 0\) → yes, sign flips. Crossing at \(x = -1\) → yes, sign flips. So signs are \(+, -, +, +\) (right to left across intervals).
Slant asymptote \(R(x) = (x^2 + 3x + 2)/(x - 1)\). Long-divide: \(R = (x + 4) + 6/(x-1)\). Slant \(y = x + 4\). For \(x \to +\infty\): \(6/(x-1) > 0\), so graph approaches from above. For \(x \to -\infty\): \(6/(x-1) < 0\), approaches from below. ✓

title: Polynomial Snaking Method; Rational Functions and Slant Asymptotes via Long Division
text:
Fragility summary


Complex roots in the denominator give no vertical asymptotes (the irreducible quadratic factor is positive everywhere or always negative).
Slant asymptote crossings. The graph can cross its slant asymptote at any zero of \(r(x)\) — important to identify.
Confidence. Both theorems: high (pre-calculus standard).
