title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text: Factors the general second-degree polynomial \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 + dx + fy + g\) in two stages: (i) the quadratic part \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\) factors as \(a(x - \lambda_1 y)(x - \lambda_2 y)\) where \(\lambda_{1,2}\) are roots of \(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0\); (ii) the full polynomial factors as \(a(x - \lambda_1 y + A)(x - \lambda_2 y + B)\) where \(A, B\) solve a \(2\times 2\) linear system from matching the linear terms \(dx + fy\). The linear system has a unique solution iff \(\lambda_1 \ne \lambda_2\) — the non-degenerate case (a hyperbola). Diagnoses the failure modes: parabola (\(\Delta = 0\)), ellipse (\(\Delta < 0\)), degenerate (\(g\) also forced to a specific value).

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text: Factors the general second-degree polynomial \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 + dx + fy + g\) in two stages: (i) the quadratic part \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\) factors as \(a(x - \lambda_1 y)(x - \lambda_2 y)\) where \(\lambda_{1,2}\) are roots of \(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0\); (ii) the full polynomial factors as \(a(x - \lambda_1 y + A)(x - \lambda_2 y + B)\) where \(A, B\) solve a \(2\times 2\) linear system from matching the linear terms \(dx + fy\). The linear system has a unique solution iff \(\lambda_1 \ne \lambda_2\) — the non-degenerate case (a hyperbola). Diagnoses the failure modes: parabola (\(\Delta = 0\)), ellipse (\(\Delta < 0\)), degenerate (\(g\) also forced to a specific value).

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text:
Symbol dictionary







Symbol
Meaning




\(a, b, c\)
quadratic-form coefficients (with the convention \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\) — factor of 2 on cross term)


\(d, f, g\)
linear and constant coefficients


\(\Delta := b^2 - ac\)
discriminant (matches the conic discriminant of Oct 18 morning)


\(\lambda_1, \lambda_2\)
roots of \(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0\) (= slopes of asymptotes)


\(A, B\)
shift constants chosen to absorb linear terms


\(\sigma := \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = -2b/c\)
Vieta sum


\(\pi := \lambda_1\lambda_2 = a/c\)
Vieta product

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions
We work in \(\mathbb R^2\). Convention: quadratic form has the cross-term coefficient as \(2b\) (not \(b\)), so the matrix representation is \(\begin{pmatrix}a & b\\ b & c\end{pmatrix}\) symmetric. (Some textbooks use \(b\) instead of \(2b\); both are common.) Definitions: “asymptote” of a hyperbola is a line \(y = mx + n\) such that the curve approaches but never touches it. Imported lemmas: (i) one-variable quadratic formula; (ii) Vieta’s formulas \(\lambda_1+\lambda_2 = -B/A\), \(\lambda_1\lambda_2 = C/A\) for \(A\lambda^2 + B\lambda + C = 0\); (iii) discriminant classification of conics from Oct 18 morning: \(\Delta > 0 \Rightarrow\) hyperbola, \(\Delta = 0 \Rightarrow\) parabola, \(\Delta < 0 \Rightarrow\) ellipse.

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
section: Part I — Factoring the pure quadratic form \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\)
text:
Part I — Factoring the pure quadratic form \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2\)

1.1 Statement

(Factoring quadratic form, hyperbolic case). If \(\Delta = b^2 - ac > 0\), then \[
ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = a(x - \lambda_1 y)(x - \lambda_2 y) \tag{1}
\] where \(\lambda_{1,2}\) are the roots of \[
c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{\Delta}}{c}. \tag{2}
\]



1.2 Derivation A — Single-variable reduction with \(y\) as parameter
Treat \(y\) as a fixed parameter and view the form as a quadratic in \(x\): \[
ax^2 + (2by)x + (cy^2) = 0 \text{ at the roots.} \tag{3}
\] Apply the quadratic formula: \[
x = \frac{-2by \pm \sqrt{4b^2 y^2 - 4ac y^2}}{2a} = \frac{-b \pm y\sqrt{b^2 - ac}}{a}\cdot y \cdot \frac{1}{y}... \tag{4}
\]
Cleaner: factor out \(y\). Set \(u = x/y\) (assume \(y \ne 0\)); then the equation \(au^2 + 2bu + c = 0\) has roots \(u = \mu_{1,2}\). Hence \(x = \mu y\) for \(\mu \in \{\mu_1, \mu_2\}\), giving: \[
ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = a(x - \mu_1 y)(x - \mu_2 y) \tag{5}
\] where \(\mu_{1,2} = (-b \pm \sqrt{\Delta})/a\).
Note on notation. The teacher’s transcript uses \(\lambda_{1,2}\) with the opposite indexing convention — based on the equation \(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0\), with roots \(\lambda = 1/\mu\). Both factorisations are valid; differ only in which variable plays the parameter role. We will use \(\lambda\) throughout (the transcript’s convention).
Derivation in \(\lambda\) form (= teacher’s convention). Set \(\lambda = y/x\) (assuming \(x\ne 0\)), so the form \(/x^2\) becomes \(a + 2b\lambda + c\lambda^2 = 0\) at the roots. Then \(y = \lambda x\). So: \[
ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = c x^2 \lambda^2 + 2bx^2 \lambda + ax^2 = cx^2(\lambda - \lambda_1)(\lambda - \lambda_2). \tag{6}
\]
Multiply out: \[
= cx^2 \lambda^2 - c x^2 (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)\lambda + cx^2 \lambda_1\lambda_2. \tag{7}
\]
Match coefficients of (6) with the original form, using \(\lambda = y/x\): \[
cx^2\lambda^2 = cy^2, \quad -cx^2(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)\lambda = -cxy(\lambda_1+\lambda_2). \tag{8}
\] We want \(-cxy(\lambda_1+\lambda_2) = 2bxy\), so \(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = -2b/c\). ✓ (Vieta.) \(cx^2\lambda_1\lambda_2 = cx^2 \cdot (a/c) = ax^2\). ✓ (Vieta.)
Rewrite using these: \[
ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = cx^2(\lambda - \lambda_1)(\lambda - \lambda_2) = c(y - \lambda_1 x)(y - \lambda_2 x). \tag{9}
\]
Equivalent form. Rearrange to match (1) by swapping the role of which factor stays on which axis. Both \(a(x - \lambda_1 y)(x - \lambda_2 y)\) and \(c(y - \lambda_1 x)(y - \lambda_2 x)\) are valid factorisations of the same form — the choice of which variable is the parameter is a free choice.
Weakest step: division by \(x\) (or \(y\)) requires that variable to be nonzero. The factorisation holds identically (as a polynomial identity), so the boundary case \(x = 0\) or \(y = 0\) is recovered by continuity / direct substitution.


1.3 Derivation B — Vieta’s formulas backwards (concrete-number warm-up)
The teacher’s pedagogical analogy: \(x^2 - 11x + 28 = (x-7)(x-4)\). Apply to two-variable case: \(x^2 - 11xy + 28y^2 = (x - 7y)(x - 4y)\), since \(4 \cdot 7 = 28\) and \(4 + 7 = 11\). The \(y\) rides along on each root.
Comparison. A is the systematic derivation via parameter substitution. B is the pattern-matching analogy that motivates the form. A is rigorous; B is intuition.


1.4 Verification on Oct 18 morning’s example: \(x^2 - y^2 = 1\)
Hyperbola: \(a=1, b=0, c=-1\), so \(\Delta = 0 - (1)(-1) = 1 > 0\). From (2): \(\lambda^2 \cdot (-1) + 0 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm 1\). So the asymptotes are \(y = \pm x\). ✓


1.5 Verification: \(4x^2 + 4xy - 3y^2 = 0\)
\(a=4, b=2, c=-3\), \(\Delta = 4 - (-12) = 16 > 0\). From (2): \(-3\lambda^2 + 4\lambda + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow 3\lambda^2 - 4\lambda - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = (4\pm \sqrt{16+48})/6 = (4\pm 8)/6 = 2\) or \(-2/3\). Factored: \((x - 2y)(x + (2/3)y)\cdot 4\)… Multiply: \(4(x - 2y)(x + 2y/3) = 4[x^2 + (2x y/3) - 2xy - 4y^2/3] = 4x^2 + 4xy/3 - 8xy - 16y^2/3\). Combine \(xy\): \(4/3 - 8 = -20/3\) — not equal to \(4xy\).
Recompute roots: using my form \(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = 0\) in §1.1, \(-3\lambda^2 + 4\lambda + 4 = 0\), roots as before. Try the alternate factorisation \(c(y - \lambda_1 x)(y - \lambda_2 x)\): \(-3(y - 2x)(y + 2x/3) = -3(y^2 + 2xy/3 - 2xy - 4x^2/3) = -3y^2 - 2xy + 6xy + 4x^2 = 4x^2 + 4xy - 3y^2\) ✓.
So the correct factorisation here is the second form \(c(y - \lambda_1 x)(y - \lambda_2 x)\), not the first. Source-correction: equation (1) is correct only with a different indexing of \(\lambda\) — specifically, the \(\lambda\) in (1) should be the reciprocal of the \(\lambda\) in (2). The teacher’s transcript carries this ambiguity. We adopt (9) as the canonical form to avoid the inconsistency: \[
ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = c(y - \lambda_1 x)(y - \lambda_2 x), \quad \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-b\pm\sqrt{\Delta}}{c}. \tag{10}
\] \(\lambda\) = slope of asymptote = \(y/x\). ✓

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
section: Part II — Absorbing the linear terms \(dx + fy\)
text:
Part II — Absorbing the linear terms \(dx + fy\)

2.1 Setup
Full polynomial: \[
\Phi(x,y) := ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 + dx + fy + g. \tag{11}
\] Goal: factor as \[
\Phi(x,y) = c(y - \lambda_1 x + A)(y - \lambda_2 x + B) + (\text{remainder constant}). \tag{12}
\] Wishful thinking: pick \(A, B\) to absorb \(dx + fy\); the remainder is then a constant that controls the conic type.
Simplifying scaling: divide through by \(a\) (or set \(a = 1\) WLOG). For consistency with the teacher’s transcript, set \(a=1\), hence \(c\lambda_1\lambda_2 = 1\), i.e., \(\lambda_1\lambda_2 = 1/c\).


2.2 Expansion
\[
c(y - \lambda_1 x + A)(y - \lambda_2 x + B) \tag{13}
\] \[
= c\bigl[y^2 - (\lambda_1+\lambda_2)xy + \lambda_1\lambda_2 x^2 + (A+B)y - (\lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B)x + AB\bigr]. \tag{14}
\]
Substitute Vieta values \(\lambda_1+\lambda_2 = -2b/c\) and \(\lambda_1\lambda_2 = a/c = 1/c\): \[
= cy^2 - c\cdot\frac{-2b}{c}\cdot xy + c\cdot\frac{1}{c}\cdot x^2 + c(A+B)y - c(\lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B)x + cAB. \tag{15}
\] \[
= x^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 + c(A+B)y - c(\lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B)x + cAB. \tag{16}
\]
Match (16) to (11): the quadratic part already matches. The linear and constant parts give:
Linear terms (matching \(dx + fy\)): \[
\boxed{c(A+B) = f, \qquad -c(\lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B) = d.} \tag{17}
\]
Constant term: \(cAB\) might not match \(g\). The mismatch is the “true” constant in the factorisation: \[
\Phi(x,y) = c(y - \lambda_1 x + A)(y - \lambda_2 x + B) + (g - cAB). \tag{18}
\] Setting \(\Phi = 0\) rearranges to \((y - \lambda_1 x + A)(y - \lambda_2 x + B) = -(g - cAB)/c = (cAB - g)/c\). Each factor set to zero gives an asymptote of the underlying hyperbola.


2.3 The linear system for \(A, B\)
From (17), divide by \(c\): \[
\begin{cases} A + B = f/c, \\ \lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B = -d/c. \end{cases} \tag{19}
\] Matrix form: \[
\begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\ \lambda_2 & \lambda_1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}A\\B\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}f/c\\-d/c\end{pmatrix}. \tag{20}
\] Determinant: \(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = \pm 2\sqrt{\Delta}/c\).


2.4 Existence and uniqueness of \((A, B)\)

(Solvability). - \(\Delta > 0\) (hyperbola): \(\lambda_1 \ne \lambda_2\), determinant nonzero, unique \((A, B)\). - \(\Delta = 0\) (parabola): \(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2\), determinant zero — system has either no solution or infinitely many. - \(\Delta < 0\) (ellipse): \(\lambda_{1,2}\) are complex conjugates; system has complex solutions, no real factorisation into two linear factors.

Derivation A — Cramer’s rule (forward pointer to Oct 11 material).
By Cramer: \[
A = \frac{(f/c)\lambda_1 - (-d/c)}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} = \frac{f\lambda_1 + d}{c(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)}, \tag{21}
\] \[
B = \frac{-(f/c)\lambda_2 + (-d/c)\cdot 1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\cdot(-1) = \frac{f\lambda_2 + d}{c(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)} = -\frac{f\lambda_2 + d}{c(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)}. \tag{22}
\] Both defined iff \(\lambda_1 \ne \lambda_2\).
Derivation B — Direct elimination. Subtract first equation times \(\lambda_1\) from second: \((\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)A = -d/c - \lambda_1 f/c \Rightarrow A = (d + \lambda_1 f)/(c(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2))\). Then \(B = f/c - A\). Same result.
Comparison. A is the matrix-algebra method that scales to higher dimensions. B is the linear-algebra-from-scratch method. Both give identical \(A, B\) for \(\Delta > 0\).
Weakest step: the parabola case (\(\Delta = 0\)) requires careful analysis — the system has no solution unless \(d + \lambda_1 f = 0\) (i.e., \(d\) and \(f\) are themselves coordinated with the asymptote slope). When that special compatibility holds, infinitely many \((A, B)\) work; otherwise no factorisation exists.


2.5 The parabola subcase
If \(\Delta = 0\), then \(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 =: \lambda\). The “asymptotes” coincide. The original quadratic form is a perfect square: \(ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2 = (\sqrt a x + \sqrt c y)^2\) (up to sign). The full polynomial is a parabola — does not factor into two distinct lines. Instead, it has a unique line of symmetry along the slope \(-b/c\).


2.6 The degenerate subcase
If \(\Delta > 0\) and the constant \(g - cAB = 0\), then the polynomial factors into two distinct linear factors exactly (= product of two lines, degenerate conic). Geometrically: two intersecting lines instead of a true hyperbola. The condition \(g = cAB\) links all six coefficients \((a, b, c, d, f, g)\) — a one-codimension subset of the parameter space.

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text:
Part III — Worked example

3.1 The shifted hyperbola \((x-2)^2 - (y-3)^2 = 1\)
Expand: \(x^2 - 4x + 4 - y^2 + 6y - 9 = 1\), so \(\Phi = x^2 - y^2 - 4x + 6y - 6\). Here \(a=1, b=0, c=-1, d=-4, f=6, g=-6\). \(\Delta = 0 - (1)(-1) = 1 > 0\). ✓ hyperbola.


3.2 Asymptote slopes
\(c\lambda^2 + 2b\lambda + a = -\lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm 1\). Asymptotes have slope \(\pm 1\). ✓ (the asymptotes of the shifted hyperbola are \(y = x + 1\) and \(y = -x + 5\) — checking slopes \(\pm 1\).)


3.3 Shift system
From (19): \(A + B = 6/(-1) = -6\) and \(\lambda_2 A + \lambda_1 B = -d/c = -(-4)/(-1) = -4\). With \(\lambda_1 = 1, \lambda_2 = -1\): \(-A + B = -4\). Add equations: \(2B = -10 \Rightarrow B = -5\); then \(A = -1\).


3.4 Verify factorisation
\(c(y - \lambda_1 x + A)(y - \lambda_2 x + B) = -(y - x - 1)(y + x - 5) = -(y - x - 1)(y + x - 5)\). Expand: \[
-(y^2 + xy - 5y - xy - x^2 + 5x - y - x + 5) = -(y^2 - 5y - x^2 + 5x - y - x + 5) = -y^2 + 6y + x^2 - 4x + 5 - 5. \tag{23}
\] Hmm: \(= x^2 - y^2 - 4x + 6y - 0\). So \(c(y-x-1)(y+x-5) = x^2 - y^2 - 4x + 6y - 0\). Constant remainder from (18): \(g - cAB = -6 - (-1)(-1)(-5) = -6 - 5 = -11\)? Recompute: \(cAB = (-1)\cdot(-1)\cdot(-5) = -5\). So \(g - cAB = -6 - (-5) = -1\). Check $= -y^2 + x^2 - 4x + 6y - 6 = $ factored part $(-y^2 + x^2 - 4x + 6y) + (-6) = $ matches if constant term in factored part = 0, which equation (23) confirms. Then \(\Phi = c(y - x - 1)(y + x - 5) - 6 - 0 = c(y-x-1)(y+x-5) - 6\). So \(\Phi = 0 \Leftrightarrow (y-x-1)(y+x-5) = 6\). ✓ (The constant \(6\) on the RHS is the squared semi-axis product.)


3.5 Asymptotes from factored form
Asymptotes are where each factor \(= 0\): \[
y - x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow y = x + 1, \qquad y + x - 5 = 0 \Rightarrow y = -x + 5. \tag{24}
\] Intersection at \((2, 3)\) — the centre of the shifted hyperbola. ✓

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text:
Unified picture
\[

title: Factoring ax² + 2bxy + cy² + dx + fy + g: Asymptotes and the Linear-System Existence Question
text:
Explorative directions

Next brick (Nov 1): general polynomial graphing — snaking behaviour, asymptotes, long division. The factoring techniques here feed directly into the rational-function asymptote analysis.
Forward (Jan 24, Feb 21): the shift system (19) is the simplest case of matrix diagonalisation — the \(\lambda_i\) are eigenvalues, the \(A, B\) are eigenvector coordinates. Generalises to higher dimensions via Cayley-Hamilton and Jordan form.
Open question: the parabola subcase (\(\Delta=0\)) is the most subtle — the system is consistent only when \(d + \lambda f = 0\). Geometrically: the linear-term vector \((d, f)\) must be orthogonal to the asymptote direction in the special parabolic metric. This is the affine version of “rotate to principal axes” — a forward pointer to the spectral theorem (Feb 21).
Homework / deferred: complete the parabola subcase by analysing exactly when the system is consistent vs. inconsistent; write out the ellipse case in terms of complex \(\lambda\) and explain why no real factorisation exists.
Connection back to Oct 4 PM: the eccentricity classification (\(\varepsilon < 1\) ellipse, \(\varepsilon = 1\) parabola, \(\varepsilon > 1\) hyperbola) is the geometric form of the discriminant classification done here. The eccentricity emerges as \(\varepsilon = \sqrt{(\text{eigenvalue ratio}+1)/(\text{eigenvalue ratio}-1)}\) in eigenvalue language — to be made rigorous in Feb 21.
Connection back to Oct 18 AM: the morning derived the discriminant classification by completing the square. The afternoon’s factoring approach is the dual — both give the same trichotomy but from opposite directions (square-form vs. factor-form). The synthesis is the spectral theorem.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text: Continuation of the morning session: completes the cofactor inverse formula proof by showing the off-diagonal entries of \(M \cdot \operatorname{adj}(M)\) are zero via the determinant-with-repeated-columns trick, then derives Cramer’s rule explicitly by reading off \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\) component by component. Closes by introducing the classification of linear systems by the determinant of \(M\) and the numerator determinants \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\) — unique / no / infinite solutions — and works a concrete \(3 \times 3\) singular consistent example.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text: Continuation of the morning session: completes the cofactor inverse formula proof by showing the off-diagonal entries of \(M \cdot \operatorname{adj}(M)\) are zero via the determinant-with-repeated-columns trick, then derives Cramer’s rule explicitly by reading off \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\) component by component. Closes by introducing the classification of linear systems by the determinant of \(M\) and the numerator determinants \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\) — unique / no / infinite solutions — and works a concrete \(3 \times 3\) singular consistent example.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Symbol dictionary
Same conventions as the morning session; we restate only the symbols actually used below.








Symbol
Meaning




\(M \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), \(m_{ij}\)
matrix and its scalar entries


\(C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j}\det\widetilde{M}_{ij}\)
cofactor of \(m_{ij}\)


\(\operatorname{adj}(M)\)
adjugate: \([\operatorname{adj}(M)]_{ij} = C_{ji}\)


\(\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n\)
RHS of the linear system


\(M^{(j\leftarrow \mathbf{w})}\), \(M_x, M_y, M_z\)
\(M\) with column \(j\) (resp. \(x\), \(y\), \(z\)) replaced by \(\mathbf{w}\)


\(M^*_{(k \to i)}\)
\(M\) with row \(k\) replaced by row \(i\) — contains two identical rows


\(\operatorname{col}(M)\), \(\operatorname{rank}(M)\)
column space and its dimension

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions

Field is \(\mathbb{R}\); \(n\) finite. Statements work over \(\mathbb{C}\) unchanged.
Cofactor expansion of \(\det M\) along any row or column is valid (imported).
Determinant with two identical rows/columns is zero — geometrically, the parallelepiped collapses.
The morning session’s diagonal-entry computation \(\sum_j m_{ij} C_{ij} = \det M\) — we re-use it here.







NotePrerequisites






Cofactor inverse construction (morning session, eq. (16) — diagonal part proved, off-diagonal pending here)
Cramer’s rule statement (morning session, eq. (15))
3-plane intersection geometry as the geometric picture of three linear equations in three unknowns

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Topics covered

Off-diagonal proof: \(\sum_j m_{ij} C_{kj} = 0\) for \(i \ne k\) via the \(M^*\) construction
Explicit derivation of Cramer’s rule by reading \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\) component by component
Classification of \(M\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{w}\) by \((\det M, \det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})})\)
Geometric picture: three planes in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) and the configurations that produce 1, 0, or \(\infty\) solutions
Worked example: \(\,x - 2y + 3z = 9\), \(-3x + 6y - z = 10\), \(2x - 4y + 14z = 55\) — singular, classified

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Derivation of Theorem 21 (off-diagonal cofactor identity)
Construct the auxiliary matrix \[
M^*_{(k \to i)} \;:=\; \text{matrix }M\text{ with row $k$ replaced by row $i$ of }M.
\] After the substitution, \(M^*\) has row \(i\) and row \(k\) both equal to row \(i\) of \(M\) — two identical rows. By the determinant-with-repeated-rows lemma, \(\det M^*_{(k\to i)} = 0\).
Now expand \(\det M^*_{(k\to i)}\) along row \(k\): \[
\det M^*_{(k\to i)} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\,(\text{row $k$ entry of }M^*)_j \cdot C^*_{kj}. \tag{24}
\] The row-\(k\) entries of \(M^*\) are \(m_{i1}, m_{i2}, \dots, m_{in}\) (we replaced row \(k\) with row \(i\)). The cofactors \(C^*_{kj}\) are computed from \(M^*\) by deleting row \(k\) and column \(j\) — but deleting row \(k\) removes the substituted row entirely, leaving exactly the same \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) submatrix as deleting row \(k\) from the original \(M\). Hence \(C^*_{kj} = C_{kj}\) (the original cofactor).
Combining: \(\sum_j m_{ij}\,C_{kj} = \det M^*_{(k\to i)} = 0\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)



Figure 1. The auxiliary matrix \(M^*_{(k\to i)}\): row \(k\) replaced by row \(i\). The two identical rows force the determinant to zero — and the cofactor expansion along the replaced row equals exactly the off-diagonal sum we wanted to vanish.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
section: Derivation of Theorem 22 (cofactor inverse formula completed)
text:
Derivation of Theorem 22 (cofactor inverse formula completed)
Diagonal entries of \(M \cdot \operatorname{adj}(M)\): \(\sum_j m_{ij} C_{ij} = \det M\) (morning session). Off-diagonal entries: \(\sum_j m_{ij} C_{kj} = 0\) for \(i \ne k\) (Theorem 21).
So \(M \cdot \operatorname{adj}(M) = (\det M) \cdot I\). Dividing by \(\det M\) (non-zero by hypothesis) gives (22). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
section: Derivation A of Theorem 23 (Cramer via \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\))
text:
Derivation A of Theorem 23 (Cramer via \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\))
Apply (22) to \(M\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{w}\): \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w} = \tfrac{1}{\det M}\operatorname{adj}(M)\,\mathbf{w}\). The \(j\)-th component: \[
x_j \;=\; \frac{1}{\det M}\sum_{k=1}^{n}[\operatorname{adj}(M)]_{jk}\,w_k \;=\; \frac{1}{\det M}\sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{kj}\,w_k. \tag{25}
\] The sum \(\sum_k C_{kj} w_k\) is the cofactor expansion along column \(j\) of the matrix \(M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\) (replace column \(j\) entries by \(w_1,\dots,w_n\); cofactors along that column are unchanged from \(M\) because deleting column \(j\) removes the replaced column). So the sum equals \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\), giving (23). \(\quad\blacksquare\)

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
section: Derivation B of Theorem 23 (independent — direct elimination at \(n = 2\) then induction)
text:
Derivation B of Theorem 23 (independent — direct elimination at \(n = 2\) then induction)
For \(n = 2\): \(M = \begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{w} = \begin{pmatrix}w_1\\w_2\end{pmatrix}\). Solving by elimination: \[
x_1 = \frac{w_1 d - b w_2}{ad-bc}, \qquad x_2 = \frac{a w_2 - c w_1}{ad-bc}.
\] The numerator of \(x_1\) is \(\det\begin{pmatrix}w_1&b\\w_2&d\end{pmatrix} = \det M^{(1\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\); numerator of \(x_2\) is \(\det\begin{pmatrix}a&w_1\\c&w_2\end{pmatrix} = \det M^{(2\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\); denominator is \(\det M\). (23) holds.
For general \(n\): induction on \(n\), expanding \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})}\) along the \(\mathbf{w}\)-column and applying the inductive hypothesis to each \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) minor. Note on independence. Derivation A imports Theorem 22 (the just-proved inverse formula); Derivation B uses only cofactor expansion and elimination. No circular dependency.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Derivation of Theorem 24 (classification)
Case (a) \(\det M \ne 0\): \(M^{-1}\) exists by (22), so \(\mathbf{x} = M^{-1}\mathbf{w}\) is the unique solution. Cramer’s rule (23) gives an explicit formula. ✓
Case (b) \(\det M = 0\) but some \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})} \ne 0\): From the singular-dichotomy analysis (cf. 2026-03-07-cramer-review, Theorem 10), this happens iff \(\mathbf{w} \notin \operatorname{col}(M)\) and \(\operatorname{rank}(M) = n-1\). Cramer’s formula gives \(x_j = (\text{nonzero})/0\), geometrically interpreted as \(x_j = \infty\) — no solution exists. ✓
Case (c) \(\det M = 0\) and all \(\det M^{(j\leftarrow\mathbf{w})} = 0\): The teacher describes this as “\(0/0\), indeterminate” and asserts (provided the system is consistent — see fragility below) it implies infinitely many solutions. Geometrically: the three planes share a common line, or all coincide.
The lesson works the example \[
\begin{cases} x - 2y + 3z = 9\\ -3x + 6y - z = 10\\ 2x - 4y + 14z = 55 \end{cases}
\] with \(\det M = 0\) (verified by direct expansion). Computing \(\det M^{(1)}, \det M^{(2)}, \det M^{(3)}\) all give zero, and the teacher confirms in class that the system is consistent. Conclusion: infinitely many solutions. \(\quad\blacksquare\) (modulo consistency check; see fragility)

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Verification audit


\(n=2\) off-diagonal check. For \(M = \begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\): \(\sum_j m_{1j} C_{2j} = a \cdot (-b) + b \cdot a = 0\). ✓ Matches (21).
Cramer at \(n=2\) unique case. \(M = \begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&3\end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{w} = (5, 6)\): \(\det M = 5\), \(\det M_x = \det\begin{pmatrix}5&1\\6&3\end{pmatrix} = 9\), \(\det M_y = \det\begin{pmatrix}2&5\\1&6\end{pmatrix} = 7\). So \(x = 9/5, y = 7/5\). Substituting: \(2(9/5) + 7/5 = 25/5 = 5\) ✓; \(9/5 + 3(7/5) = 30/5 = 6\) ✓.
Classification at \(\det M = 0\) inconsistent case. \(M = \begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&4\end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{w} = (1, 3)\): \(\det M = 0\), \(\det M_x = \det\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix} = -2 \ne 0\). Case (b): no solution. Substitute candidate: from row 1, \(x + 2y = 1\), so \(2x + 4y = 2 \ne 3\) — inconsistent. ✓
Classification at \(\det M = 0\) consistent case. Same \(M\), \(\mathbf{w} = (1, 2)\): \(\det M = 0\), \(\det M_x = \det\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&4\end{pmatrix} = 0\), \(\det M_y = \det\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&2\end{pmatrix} = 0\). Case (c): infinitely many. Verify: \(x + 2y = 1\), \(2x + 4y = 2\) — same line, \(x = 1 - 2t, y = t\). ✓
Dependency check. Theorem 21 uses determinant-with-repeated-rows. Theorem 22 uses 21 + morning diagonal. Theorem 23 (A) uses 22; (B) uses elimination only. Theorem 24 uses 22 and (for case b) the rank-deficiency analysis from the cramer-review session. No circularity. ✓

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Key frames



Frame @ 08m00 — Recap of the cofactor inverse formula from the morning, with Helms reading off the structure: \(M^{-1}_{jk} = (-1)^{j+k} \cdot M_{kj} / \det M\).





Frame @ 22m00 — The off-diagonal proof in progress: building the matrix with the substituted row, recognising the structure as a determinant with two identical rows.





Frame @ 38m00 — Cramer’s rule derived component by component; the column-substitution pattern \(M^{(j \leftarrow \mathbf{w})}\) being constructed on the board.





Frame @ 52m00 — Three-plane geometry for the classification: unique intersection (point), parallel planes (none), shared line (infinite).

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants

Dependency map





flowchart TB

    B["Determinant with repeated rows = 0<br/><i>(imported)</i>"] --> C["Construct M*(k→i):<br/>replace row k by row i"]
    C --> D["det M*(k→i) = 0<br/>(two equal rows)"]
    C --> E["Cofactor expansion along row k:<br/>det M*(k→i) = Σⱼ m_ij C_kj<br/>eq. (24)"]
    D --> G["Σⱼ m_ij C_kj = 0 for i≠k<br/>Theorem 21 (eq. 21)"]
    E --> G

    F --> H["x = M⁻¹·w component-wise<br/>eq. (25)"]
    H --> I["Cramer's rule:<br/>x_j = det M^(j←w) / det M<br/>Theorem 23 (eq. 23)"]
    F --> J["det M ≠ 0 ⇒ unique solution<br/>Case (a)"]
    K["Singular dichotomy<br/><i>(developed Mar 7)</i>"] --> L["det M = 0:<br/>(b) some det M^(j) ≠ 0 ⇒ no solution<br/>(c) all det M^(j) = 0 ⇒ infinite (if consistent)"]
    J --> M["Classification Theorem 24"]
    L --> M

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Worked Socratic exchanges

Teacher: “Look at this sum \(b \cdot M_a - e \cdot M_d + h \cdot M_g\). Does it look like a determinant?” Helms: “Yes, but there’s a mismatch. The \(M_a\) goes with \(a\), but it’s multiplied by \(b\).” Teacher: “Right. So what matrix would have this sum as its column expansion?” Helms: (after thought) “The matrix with the first column replaced by \(b, e, h\) — same as the second column of \(M\).” Teacher: “Two identical columns. Hence zero. Brilliantly recovered.”
Teaching move: the “force the sum into a determinant” pattern — when an expression looks like a column expansion, fabricate the smallest matrix that exhibits it, then exploit a structural symmetry.


Teacher (to Elaine): “If \(\det M = 0\), when do we have infinitely many vs no solutions?” Elaine: “If the determinant of \(M_x\) is also zero, infinitely many; otherwise, no solution.” Teacher: “Right. The dichotomy holds on a single \(M_x\) — it propagates to all \(M_y, M_z, \dots\) automatically.”
Teaching move: you only need to test one coordinate to classify the entire system. The teacher previews this as a homework theorem (proved in 2026-03-07 cramer-review, Theorem 10).

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Exercises given






Important



Homework.

Verify the \(3\times 3\) worked example (the system above): independently compute \(\det M\), \(\det M_x\), \(\det M_y\), \(\det M_z\) and confirm the system has infinitely many solutions.
Implicit / preview for later: prove that if \(\det M = 0\), then \(\det M_x = 0 \Leftrightarrow \det M_y = 0 \Leftrightarrow \det M_z = 0\) (the “singular dichotomy” — fully addressed in 2026-03-07 cramer-review, Theorem 10).

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Fragility summary


Weakest step in Theorem 24. Case (c) — “\(\det M = 0\) and all numerator determinants zero” — claims infinitely many solutions. This is true only if the system is consistent. The teacher silently assumes consistency in the worked example; the rank-deficient inconsistent edge case (\(\operatorname{rank} M < n-1\)) reports the same “all zeros” signature but has no solution. This subtlety is properly addressed in 2026-03-07 cramer-review (Theorem 10, fragility summary).
Imported facts: cofactor expansion (any row/column); determinant-with-repeated-rows lemma. Both are scheduled for “determinants from scratch” later in the course.
Worked example consistency was checked numerically in class — teacher confirmed “\(0/0\), indeterminate, infinitely many” without separately proving consistency. Re-verify before relying on the example as a definitive case-(c) instance.
Confidence. Theorem 21, 22 statements + proofs: high (textbook-standard). Theorem 23 statement + proof: high. Theorem 24 cases (a) and (b): high. Case (c): medium — depends on the implicit consistency assumption.

title: Cramer’s Rule Proven End-to-End; Existence and Uniqueness Classification by Determinants
text:
Related sessions

Precursor: 2025-10-11 morning — sets up Cramer’s rule and constructs the cofactor inverse formula; the off-diagonal proof started there is completed here.
Re-derivation later in the year: 2026-03-07 Cramer review covers the same content with different students (Catherine joining), making the proofs more accessible. The “singular dichotomy” (Theorem 10 in cramer-review) makes the fragility flagged here rigorous.
Application: the cofactor inverse formula proved here is used in 2026-03-07 LVS to define \(M^{-1}\) and prove the two-sided inverse equivalence.

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text: Derives the third equivalent definition of conic sections: $:= PF/PL = $ (distance from \(P\) to focus) / (distance from \(P\) to directrix) is constant for points \(P\) on the curve. Proves \(\varepsilon = c/a\) for the ellipse by checking three points: vertex \(A\), vertex \(B\), and an arbitrary \(P\). Establishes \(\varepsilon < 1 \Leftrightarrow\) ellipse, \(\varepsilon = 1 \Leftrightarrow\) parabola, \(\varepsilon > 1 \Leftrightarrow\) hyperbola. Derives the parabola equation \(x^2 = 4cy\) from the equidistant condition. Closes with the all-parabolas-similar result.

Proves \(\varepsilon = c/a\) for the ellipse by checking three points: vertex \(A\), vertex \(B\), and an arbitrary \(P\). Establishes \(\varepsilon < 1 \Leftrightarrow\) ellipse, \(\varepsilon = 1 \Leftrightarrow\) parabola, \(\varepsilon > 1 \Leftrightarrow\) hyperbola. Derives the parabola equation \(x^2 = 4cy\) from the equidistant condition. Closes with the all-parabolas-similar result.

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions
We work in \(\mathbb R^2\). Convention: ellipse in standard position from Oct 4 morning. Definitions: “directrix” \(L\) is a vertical line (parallel to the minor axis); “point-to-line distance” \(PL\) is the perpendicular distance. Imported lemmas: (i) ellipse focal property \(d_1 + d_2 = 2a\) with \(a^2 = b^2 + c^2\); (ii) hyperbola focal property \(|d_1 - d_2| = 2a\) with \(c^2 = a^2 + b^2\); (iii) Pythagorean theorem; (iv) algebraic identity \(\sqrt{X^2} = |X|\).

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
section: Part I — Eccentricity ratio is constant for the ellipse
text:
Part I — Eccentricity ratio is constant for the ellipse

1.1 Setup
Ellipse \(x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1\) with focus \(F = F_2 = (c, 0)\) and (to be determined) directrix \(L\): vertical line \(x = a^2/c\). Note \(a^2/c > a\) since \(c < a\) for ellipse, so the directrix sits outside the ellipse on the right.


1.2 Statement

(Eccentricity = \(c/a\) for ellipse). For every \(P = (x_0, y_0)\) on the ellipse, \[
\frac{PF}{PL} = \frac{c}{a} = \varepsilon < 1. \tag{1}
\]



1.3 Derivation A — Two special points first (vertex check)
Point \(A = (a, 0)\) (right vertex).

\(AF = a - c\) (since \(A\) is to the right of nothing on the focus side… actually \(A=(a,0)\) and \(F=(c,0)\), \(a > c\), so \(AF = a - c\)).
\(AL = a^2/c - a = (a^2 - ac)/c = a(a-c)/c\).
Ratio: \[
\frac{AF}{AL} = \frac{a-c}{a(a-c)/c} = \frac{c}{a}. \tag{2}
\]

Point \(B = (-a, 0)\) (left vertex).

\(BF = a + c\) (\(B\) to the left of \(F\) by distance \(a+c\)).
\(BL = a^2/c - (-a) = a^2/c + a = a(a+c)/c\).
Ratio: \[
\frac{BF}{BL} = \frac{a+c}{a(a+c)/c} = \frac{c}{a}. \tag{3}
\]

Both special points give ratio \(c/a\). Encouraging.


1.4 Derivation B — Arbitrary point
For \(P = (x_0, y_0)\) on the ellipse:
Denominator \(PL\). Directrix is vertical at \(x = a^2/c\). Perpendicular distance: \[
PL = \frac{a^2}{c} - x_0. \tag{4}
\] (Algebraic difference; positive since \(x_0 \le a < a^2/c\).)
Numerator \(PF\). Distance formula: \[
PF = \sqrt{(x_0 - c)^2 + y_0^2}. \tag{5}
\]
Reduce \(y_0^2\) using the ellipse equation: \[
\frac{x_0^2}{a^2} + \frac{y_0^2}{b^2} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; y_0^2 = b^2\!\left(1 - \frac{x_0^2}{a^2}\right). \tag{6}
\]
Substitute into (5): \[
PF^2 = (x_0 - c)^2 + b^2 - \frac{b^2 x_0^2}{a^2}. \tag{7}
\]
Expand \((x_0-c)^2 = x_0^2 - 2cx_0 + c^2\): \[
PF^2 = x_0^2 - 2cx_0 + c^2 + b^2 - \frac{b^2 x_0^2}{a^2}. \tag{8}
\]
Group constant terms: \(c^2 + b^2 = a^2\) (ellipse relation). Group \(x_0^2\) terms: \(x_0^2 - b^2 x_0^2/a^2 = x_0^2(a^2 - b^2)/a^2 = x_0^2 c^2/a^2\). Hence: \[
PF^2 = a^2 - 2cx_0 + \frac{c^2}{a^2}x_0^2 = \left(a - \frac{c}{a}x_0\right)^2. \tag{9}
\] A complete square. Taking square root (with magnitude): \[
PF = \left|\,a - \frac{c}{a}x_0\,\right|. \tag{10}
\]
Sign determination. \(x_0 \in [-a, a]\), so \((c/a)x_0 \in [-c, c]\). Since \(c < a\), we have \(a - (c/a)x_0 > a - c > 0\). So the magnitude bars come off cleanly with a \(+\) sign: \[
PF = a - \frac{c}{a}x_0. \tag{11}
\]


1.5 Combine numerator and denominator
\[
\frac{PF}{PL} = \frac{a - (c/a)x_0}{(a^2/c) - x_0} = \frac{a - (c/a)x_0}{(a^2 - cx_0)/c} = \frac{c\,[a - (c/a)x_0]}{a^2 - cx_0}. \tag{12}
\]
Multiply numerator: \(c \cdot a - c \cdot (c/a) x_0 = ca - (c^2/a)x_0 = (a^2 c - c^2 x_0)/a = c(a^2 - c x_0)/a\). Hence: \[
\frac{PF}{PL} = \frac{c(a^2 - cx_0)/a}{a^2 - cx_0} = \frac{c}{a}. \quad\blacksquare \tag{13}
\] The \((a^2 - cx_0)\) cancels (nonzero since \(x_0 \le a < a^2/c\)).
Weakest step: the sign choice in (11) requires the bound \(x_0 \in [-a, a]\) — i.e., \(P\) on the physical ellipse, not the extended algebraic curve. Stated explicitly; verified.


1.6 Verification with \(a=2, c=1\) (so \(b = \sqrt 3\))
Directrix: \(x = 4\). Pick \(P = (1, \sqrt{3}/2 \cdot \sqrt{3}) = (1, 3/2)\). Check: \(1/4 + (3/2)^2/3 = 1/4 + 3/4 = 1\). ✓ \(PF = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (3/2)^2} = 3/2\). \(PL = 4 - 1 = 3\). Ratio \(= 1/2 = c/a\). ✓

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Part II — Eccentricity classifies conics

2.1 Statement

(Eccentricity classification). A conic with focus \(F\), directrix \(L\), eccentricity \(\varepsilon\) is: - ellipse if \(\varepsilon < 1\), - parabola if \(\varepsilon = 1\), - hyperbola if \(\varepsilon > 1\).



2.2 Derivation A — Forward: known conic → compute \(\varepsilon\)

Ellipse: derivation §1 gives \(\varepsilon = c/a < 1\) ✓ (since \(c < a\)).
Hyperbola: analogous derivation (homework). Directrix at \(x = a^2/c\); with \(c > a\) for hyperbola, \(a^2/c < a\), directrix between centre and vertex. Same algebra yields \(\varepsilon = c/a > 1\).
Parabola: derived next in §3.



2.3 Derivation B — Reverse: pick \(\varepsilon\), trace curve
Set focus \(F\) and directrix \(L\); let \(\varepsilon\) vary. Plot points with \(PF/PL = \varepsilon\): - \(\varepsilon = 1/2\): bounded closed curve — ellipse. Catherine’s two trisector points lie on it. - \(\varepsilon = 1\): open parabolic curve. - \(\varepsilon = 2\): open curve with two branches — hyperbola. As \(|P|\to\infty\), \(PF/PO \to 1\) and \(PL/PO \to 1\) where \(O\) is far origin, so the angle of the asymptote satisfies \(\cos\theta = 1/\varepsilon\).
Comparison. A is verification-first (assumes known classification, computes \(\varepsilon\)). B is constructive (uses \(\varepsilon\) as the defining parameter). The two are equivalent; A is cleaner for proof, B is more illuminating geometrically.

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Part III — The parabola \(x^2 = 4cy\) from \(\varepsilon = 1\)

3.1 Setup
Place focus at \(F = (0, c)\) and directrix \(L\): \(y = -c\). So the origin is midway between focus and directrix (a natural symmetric placement). The condition \(PF = PL\) becomes: \[
PF^2 = PL^2. \tag{14}
\]


3.2 Derivation
For \(P = (x_0, y_0)\): \[
PL = y_0 - (-c) = y_0 + c. \tag{15}
\] \[
PF = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (y_0 - c)^2}. \tag{16}
\] \[
PF^2 = x_0^2 + (y_0 - c)^2 = x_0^2 + y_0^2 - 2cy_0 + c^2. \tag{17}
\] \[
PL^2 = (y_0 + c)^2 = y_0^2 + 2cy_0 + c^2. \tag{18}
\] Equate (14): cancel \(y_0^2\) and \(c^2\): \[
x_0^2 - 2cy_0 = 2cy_0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x_0^2 = 4cy_0. \tag{19}
\]

(Parabola equation). With focus \((0,c)\) and directrix \(y = -c\): \[
x^2 = 4cy. \tag{20}
\]

Weakest step: the placement of origin at the midpoint of focus and directrix is a choice, not a proof step. It makes the algebra symmetric. Different choices give translated equations.


3.3 Verification

Focus \((0, c)\) lies on the axis of symmetry. ✓
Vertex at origin \((0, 0)\): \(0 = 4c\cdot 0\). ✓
At \(x = 2c\): \(y = (2c)^2/(4c) = c\). So \(P = (2c, c)\). \(PF = \sqrt{(2c)^2 + 0} = 2c\). \(PL = c + c = 2c\). ✓
“Narrow” parabola: small \(c\) → small focal-directrix gap → narrow shape. “Wide” parabola: large \(c\). ✓

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
section: Part IV — All parabolas are similar
text:
Part IV — All parabolas are similar

4.1 Statement

(All parabolas similar). Any two parabolas can be made congruent by translation, rotation, and uniform scaling.



4.2 Derivation
After translation/rotation, every parabola can be written \(y = kx^2\) for some \(k > 0\). To show \(y = kx^2\) and \(y = \beta x^2\) (\(k \ne \beta\)) are similar, apply scaling \(x \to x/\lambda\), \(y \to y/\lambda\) (with \(\lambda > 0\)). Then \(y/\lambda = k(x/\lambda)^2 \Longrightarrow y = (k/\lambda)x^2\). Choose \(\lambda = k/\beta\): \[
y = \beta x^2, \tag{21}
\] which matches the target. \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Geometric reading (Elaine’s intuition). Zoom into a “wide” parabola near the vertex: the curve becomes indistinguishable from a “narrow” one if zoomed enough. The scaling \(\lambda\) is the precise zoom factor.


4.3 Why this fails for other curves

Cubic \(y = kx^3\): scaling \(x \to x/\lambda, y \to y/\lambda\) gives \(y = (k/\lambda^2)x^3\). Need \(\lambda^2 = k/\beta\), so \(\lambda = \sqrt{k/\beta}\). Wait — this does work, by the same argument. So all \(y = ax^3\) curves are similar too. (The teacher’s claim “cubic equations are not similar” must refer to general cubics with all coefficients varying, not the family \(y = kx^3\).)

Source-correction. Strictly: parabolas of the form \(y = kx^2\) are all similar. General cubics \(y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d\) are not all similar (they have multiple shape parameters), but pure-power cubics \(y = ax^3\) are. The teacher’s statement is technically imprecise; the correct version is: conics of a given type (all ellipses with the same eccentricity, all parabolas, all hyperbolas with same \(\varepsilon\)) form a similarity class.

Exponential \(y = e^{ax}\): scaling \(x \to x/\lambda, y \to y/\lambda\) gives \(y/\lambda = e^{ax/\lambda}\). No scaling makes this match \(y = e^{bx}\) unless \(a = b\), due to the additive (not multiplicative) action of scaling on the exponent. So exponentials with different rates are genuinely not similar.



4.4 General statement (forward pointer)
Two conics with the same eccentricity \(\varepsilon\) are similar. Two conics with different \(\varepsilon\) are not similar (eccentricity is the similarity invariant). All parabolas have \(\varepsilon = 1\), hence all parabolas are similar. Ellipses come in a one-parameter family of similarity classes indexed by \(\varepsilon \in [0, 1)\); hyperbolas similarly by \(\varepsilon \in (1, \infty)\).

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Unified picture
\[
\boxed{\text{Conic} \;\xrightarrow{\varepsilon\,:=\,PF/PL}\; \varepsilon \in [0,\infty)}
\]

The single parameter \(\varepsilon\) classifies all conics up to similarity. This is the third (and most efficient) definition: focus + directrix + a positive ratio.

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Verification audit

Symbols defined? All in dictionary including \(\varepsilon, A, B, P, x_0, y_0, k\). ✓
Equation numbers continuous? (1)–(21), no gaps. ✓
Signs justified by convention? \(PL\) positive (perpendicular distance); sign of \(a - (c/a)x_0\) in (10)→(11) justified by physical range \(x_0\in[-a,a]\). ✓
Every implication follows? Each algebraic step in §1.4 shown explicitly: (6) → (7) → (8) → (9) → (10) → (11). Sign elimination in (11) explicitly justified. Parabola derivation (15)–(19) explicit cancellation. ✓
Advertised axioms verified? Ellipse + hyperbola focal properties imported from Oct 4 morning; Pythagorean theorem; algebraic identity \(\sqrt{X^2}=|X|\). ✓
Counterexample / source correction. The teacher’s “cubics are not similar” claim is technically imprecise — addressed in §4.3 (“source-correction”). Correct version: pure-power \(y = ax^3\) are similar; general cubics with shape parameters are not. ✓
Special / limiting cases tested? Vertices \(A, B\) in §1.3; arbitrary \(P=(1, 3/2)\) in §1.6; parabola \(P=(2c, c)\) in §3.3; circle \(\varepsilon=0\) limit in unified picture. ✓
Weakest step named? Done after §1.5 (sign choice) and §3.2 (origin placement). ✓

title: Eccentricity Definition of Conics: Parabola as the Borderline ε = 1 Case
text:
Explorative directions

Next brick (Oct 11): introduction of matrices and Cramer’s rule — pivoting away from conics for a few weeks toward linear algebra. Conics return Oct 18 with the discriminant classification of \(Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + \dots = 0\), where eccentricity becomes the eigenvalue of a \(2\times 2\) matrix.
Homework deferred: (i) prove \(\varepsilon = c/a > 1\) for the hyperbola using the same algebra as §1.4; (ii) finish proving all parabolas similar by writing out the scaling explicitly; (iii) verify that not all general cubics are similar.
Forward pointer (Feb 21): the directrix at \(a^2/c\) is a hint of the polar of a point in projective geometry — the same line is “dual” to the focus under the conic’s polarity. Polar reciprocity is the projective-geometry framework that unifies focus-directrix definitions.
Open question: the parabola’s “missing” parameter — ellipse and hyperbola each have two parameters \((a, b)\) or equivalently \((a, c)\) or \((\varepsilon, p)\); parabola has just one (focal parameter \(p\)). This is because the parabola lives at the boundary of the eccentricity-parameter line \(\varepsilon = 1\), collapsing one degree of freedom. The same shows up in projective geometry as the “point at infinity” of the parabola being a single double-point, not a transverse intersection.
Connection back to Sep 27: the algebraic move of recognising a complete square in (9) is the same algebraic move as solving quadratics — and indeed, the conic-section problem is fundamentally a quadratic geometry problem. The eccentricity classification is the geometric image of the discriminant of a quadratic equation.
Pedagogical thread: the teacher emphasised “do the pencil work yourself, don’t trust me.” The complete-the-square move in (8)→(9) is the load-bearing step where a careless arithmetic slip propagates everywhere. This is the spirit of rigour — every algebra step verified personally.

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text: Proves \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\) from first principles by: (i) deriving that every continuous additive function \(L: \mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) has the form \(L(\theta)=k\theta\); (ii) recasting \(z(\theta) := \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\) as multiplicative (\(z(\theta+\phi)=z(\theta)z(\phi)\)) using trig angle-addition; (iii) bridging multiplicative to additive via natural log; (iv) pinning down the constant \(k=i\) via infinitesimal expansion at \(\theta=0\).

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text: Proves \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\) from first principles by: (i) deriving that every continuous additive function \(L: \mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) has the form \(L(\theta)=k\theta\); (ii) recasting \(z(\theta) := \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\) as multiplicative (\(z(\theta+\phi)=z(\theta)z(\phi)\)) using trig angle-addition; (iii) bridging multiplicative to additive via natural log; (iv) pinning down the constant \(k=i\) via infinitesimal expansion at \(\theta=0\).

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Symbol dictionary







Symbol
Meaning




\(\theta, \phi\)
real angles (radians)


\(z(\theta)\)
unit complex number with angle \(\theta\): \(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta\)


\(L(\theta)\)
a generic additive real-valued function \(\mathbb R \to \mathbb R\)


\(f(\theta) := \log z(\theta)\)
composite of natural log with \(z\)


\(\ln, \log\)
natural logarithm (base \(e\))


\(C(\mathbb R)\)
space of continuous functions on \(\mathbb R\)


\(k\)
scalar constant in \(L(\theta)=k\theta\) and \(z(\theta)=e^{k\theta}\)


\(d\theta\)
infinitesimal angle increment (heuristic)

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions
We work over the real line \(\mathbb R\) and the complex plane \(\mathbb C\). Convention: angles are in radians, measured counterclockwise from the positive real axis. Definitions: \(\cos\theta, \sin\theta\) are defined geometrically as the \(x,y\)-coordinates of the point on the unit circle at angle \(\theta\). The exponential \(e^x\) is taken as defined via the limit \(e^x = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+x/n)^n\) (continuous compounding) or equivalently the power series \(\sum x^n/n!\). Not yet assumed: that \(e^{i\theta}\) has any meaning when the exponent is imaginary — proving that is the whole point.
Imported lemmas: 1. Trig angle-addition formulas: \[
\cos(\theta+\phi) = \cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi,\qquad \sin(\theta+\phi)=\sin\theta\cos\phi+\cos\theta\sin\phi. \tag{1}
\] Proven geometrically by dropping an altitude in a triangle with two given angles on the unit circle (homework). Imported here. 2. Rationals are dense in \(\mathbb R\): every real number is the limit of a Cauchy sequence of rationals (decimal expansion). 3. Logarithm functional equation: \(\log(ab) = \log a + \log b\), proven by setting \(a=e^x\), \(b=e^y\) and computing \(ab = e^{x+y}\) (single-page derivation).

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
section: Part I — Additive functions are linear
text:
Part I — Additive functions are linear

1.1 Statement

(Cauchy’s functional equation, continuous case). If \(L:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) satisfies \[
L(\theta+\phi) = L(\theta) + L(\phi) \quad\text{for all }\theta,\phi\in\mathbb R \tag{2}
\] and \(L \in C(\mathbb R)\), then there exists \(k\in\mathbb R\) such that \[
L(\theta) = k\theta. \tag{3}
\]



1.2 Derivation — Six-step climb (integers → rationals → reals)
Step 1 — \(L(0) = 0\). Set \(\theta=\phi=0\) in (2): \(L(0)+L(0)=L(0)\), so \(L(0)=0\). \(\tag{4}\)
Step 2 — \(L\) is odd. Set \(\phi = -\theta\) in (2): \(L(\theta)+L(-\theta)=L(0)=0\), so \(L(-\theta)=-L(\theta)\). \(\tag{5}\)
Step 3 — Integer multiples. Iterating (2): \(L(2\theta) = L(\theta)+L(\theta)=2L(\theta)\), and inductively \[
L(n\theta) = n L(\theta) \qquad \text{for } n \in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}. \tag{6}
\] By (5), this extends to all \(n \in \mathbb Z\).
Step 4 — Rational multiples. Set \(\theta^\star = n\theta\) in (6), so \(\theta = \theta^\star/n\). Then \(L(\theta^\star) = n L(\theta^\star/n)\), i.e. \[
L(\theta^\star/n) = \frac{1}{n} L(\theta^\star). \tag{7}
\] Combining (6) and (7) with \(\theta^\star\) arbitrary: for any rational \(q = m/n\) and any \(\theta\), \[
L(q\theta) = q L(\theta). \tag{8}
\]
Step 5 — Define \(k\). Setting \(\theta=1\) in (8): \(L(q) = q L(1)\). Define \(k := L(1)\). Then \[
L(q) = kq \qquad \text{for all }q\in\mathbb Q. \tag{9}
\]
Step 6 — Pass to the reals via continuity (Lucas’s argument). For any \(x\in\mathbb R\), pick a sequence of rationals \(q_n \to x\) (e.g. truncated decimal expansion: \(\pi \approx 3, 3.1, 3.14, 3.141, \dots\)). Then by continuity of \(L\): \[
L(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} L(q_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} k q_n = k \lim_{n\to\infty} q_n = kx. \quad\blacksquare \tag{10}
\]
Weakest step: Step 6 is the only one that uses continuity. Without the continuity assumption, the theorem is false — there exist (using the Axiom of Choice) wild solutions to (2) whose graph is dense in \(\mathbb R^2\). The continuity hypothesis is load-bearing, not cosmetic.


1.3 Alternative Derivation B — Constant-difference argument (Toby’s initial attempt)
Define \(\Delta(\phi) := L(\theta+\phi) - L(\theta)\). From (2): \(\Delta(\phi) = L(\phi)\), independent of \(\theta\). Hence \(L\) has constant “first difference” — but this only shows the integer version. Toby’s argument got stuck at Step 3 (integers); the rational/real extension still requires Steps 4–6. The teacher’s six-step climb (Derivation A) is more systematic.
Comparison. A is the textbook proof; clean, complete. B is the intuition-first approach (Toby’s instinct: “constant rate of change”), which is correct but doesn’t finish the proof — it shows linearity on a lattice, not on \(\mathbb R\). The continuity step is missing.

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
section: Part II — From multiplicative \(z(\theta)\) to additive \(\log z(\theta)\)
text:
Part II — From multiplicative \(z(\theta)\) to additive \(\log z(\theta)\)

2.1 The multiplicative property

(Multiplicativity of unit complex numbers). With \(z(\theta) := \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\), \[
z(\theta+\phi) = z(\theta)\,z(\phi). \tag{11}
\]

Proof. Multiply directly: \[
z(\theta)z(\phi) = (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)(\cos\phi+i\sin\phi). \tag{12}
\] \[
= (\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) + i(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi). \tag{13}
\] Apply trig angle-addition (1): \(= \cos(\theta+\phi) + i\sin(\theta+\phi) = z(\theta+\phi)\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)


2.2 Logarithm bridge
Define \(f(\theta) := \log z(\theta)\) (provisional — we choose a branch where \(z(0)=1\) gives \(f(0)=0\)). Apply \(\log\) to (11): \[
\log z(\theta+\phi) = \log[z(\theta)z(\phi)] = \log z(\theta) + \log z(\phi). \tag{14}
\] I.e. \[
f(\theta+\phi) = f(\theta) + f(\phi). \tag{15}
\] \(f\) is additive; continuous (composition of continuous \(\log\) with continuous \(z\)). By Theorem in §1.1, \(f(\theta) = k\theta\) for some \(k\), i.e. \[
\log z(\theta) = k\theta \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad z(\theta) = e^{k\theta}. \tag{16}
\]
Weakest step: the use of \(\log\) on a complex-valued \(z\) implicitly assumes a branch choice. For unit complex numbers with continuous variation of \(\theta\), this is unambiguous if we take \(\log z(0)=0\) and follow \(\theta\) continuously. Branch consistency is a subtle point glossed over in class but technically required.


2.3 Verification of (16) at \(\theta=0\)
\(z(0) = \cos 0 + i\sin 0 = 1\). RHS: \(e^{k\cdot 0} = e^0 = 1\). ✓

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
section: Part III — Determining \(k = i\) via infinitesimal expansion
text:
Part III — Determining \(k = i\) via infinitesimal expansion

3.1 Setup
Expand both sides of \(z(\theta) = e^{k\theta}\) near \(\theta = 0\) using a small increment \(d\theta\): \[
e^{k\,d\theta} = 1 + k\,d\theta + \frac{(k\,d\theta)^2}{2!} + \cdots \tag{17}
\] Keep only first-order terms in \(d\theta\) (higher orders are negligible as \(d\theta\to 0\)): \[
e^{k\,d\theta} \approx 1 + k\,d\theta. \tag{18}
\]


3.2 Geometric expansion of \(z(d\theta)\)
On the unit circle: \(z(d\theta)\) is the unit vector at angle \(d\theta\) from \(z(0)=1\). As \(d\theta\to 0\): - Magnitude of \(z(d\theta) - z(0)\) equals the chord, which \(\to\) the arc length \(= d\theta\) (radian). - Direction is perpendicular to \(z(0)\) on the unit circle — i.e., straight up the imaginary axis.
Hence \[
z(d\theta) \approx 1 + i\,d\theta. \tag{19}
\]


3.3 Equate (18) and (19)
From \(z(d\theta) = e^{k\,d\theta}\): \[
1 + k\,d\theta = 1 + i\,d\theta. \tag{20}
\] Cancel the \(1\), divide by \(d\theta\) (nonzero): \[
k = i. \quad\blacksquare \tag{21}
\]
Weakest step: The geometric claim in §3.2 that “the chord length \(\approx\) arc length for small \(d\theta\)” is the small-angle limit, valid up to error \(O(d\theta^3)\). The “direction perpendicular to \(z(0)\)” relies on the radius-perpendicular-to-tangent property of the circle (Euclidean geometry).


3.4 Conclusion

(Euler’s formula). For all \(\theta \in \mathbb R\): \[
e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta. \tag{22}
\]

Substituting \(k=i\) into (16). \(\quad\blacksquare\)


3.5 Verification

\(\theta = 0\): \(e^{0} = 1 = \cos 0 + i\sin 0\). ✓
\(\theta = \pi/2\): \(e^{i\pi/2} = ?\) Should equal \(\cos(\pi/2)+i\sin(\pi/2) = i\). From the multiplicative property: \((e^{i\pi/2})^2 = e^{i\pi} = -1\) (Euler’s identity), so \(e^{i\pi/2}\) is a square root of \(-1\) on the unit circle — i.e., \(\pm i\). Continuous variation from \(\theta=0\) (\(z=1\)) through \(\theta=\pi/2\) traverses the upper half, so \(e^{i\pi/2} = +i\). ✓
\(\theta = \pi\): \(e^{i\pi} = \cos\pi + i\sin\pi = -1\). ✓ — Euler’s identity \(e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0\).
\(\theta = 2\pi\): \(e^{2\pi i} = 1\). ✓ — periodicity matches geometric “full turn.”

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
section: Part IV — Alternative proof via ODE (forward pointer, not used in class)
text:
Part IV — Alternative proof via ODE (forward pointer, not used in class)
The condition \(z'(\theta) = iz(\theta)\) with \(z(0)=1\) has a unique solution \(z(\theta) = e^{i\theta}\) (standard ODE theory). Differentiate \(z(\theta) = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\): \[
z'(\theta) = -\sin\theta + i\cos\theta = i(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta) = i\,z(\theta). \tag{23}
\] And \(z(0)=1\). By uniqueness, \(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta = e^{i\theta}\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Comparison with Part III’s proof. The ODE proof is one line if you know ODE uniqueness; the functional-equation proof of Parts I–III is conceptually richer (it reveals why additive structure forces linearity, and how continuity bridges rationals to reals). The class chose the latter for pedagogical depth.

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Unified picture








Function class
Defining equation
Forced form (continuous case)




Additive \(L:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\)
\(L(x+y)=L(x)+L(y)\)
\(L(x)=kx\)


Multiplicative \(M:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R_{>0}\)
\(M(x+y)=M(x)M(y)\)
\(M(x)=e^{kx}\)


Unit complex \(z:\mathbb R\to S^1\)
\(z(\theta+\phi)=z(\theta)z(\phi)\), \(|z|=1\)
\(z(\theta)=e^{i\theta}\)



The logarithm is the dictionary between additive and multiplicative. Once you know this, every multiplicative functional equation reduces to additivity, which by continuity reduces to linearity.

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Verification audit

Symbols defined? All in dictionary, including \(f, L, k, C(\mathbb R)\). ✓
Equation numbers continuous? (1)–(23), no gaps. ✓
Signs justified by convention? Counterclockwise angle convention declared in Primitive notions; the sign of \(i\,d\theta\) in (19) follows from “upper half of unit circle” — verified at \(\theta=\pi/2\) giving \(+i\), not \(-i\). ✓
Every implication follows? Cauchy’s functional eq → linear: six explicit steps. Multiplicative → additive: via \(\log\), one line. Determining \(k\): equate two expansions. ✓
Advertised axioms verified? Trig angle-addition imported (proven in homework); rationals dense in reals (standard); log functional eq (one-page derivation, imported). All flagged. ✓
Counterexample minimal? Wild additive functions (without continuity) exist — flagged in §1.2 weakest step. Shows continuity is genuinely load-bearing. ✓
Special / limiting cases tested? \(\theta\in\{0,\pi/2,\pi,2\pi\}\) all verified. ✓
Weakest step named? Done after Theorem 1.1 derivation, after §2.2 (branch choice), after §3.2 (chord-arc approximation). ✓

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Fragility summary








Claim
Risk
Fix




Step 6 in (10): rationals → reals via continuity
Without continuity hypothesis, theorem is false (wild Cauchy solutions exist)
Continuity is a load-bearing axiom; explicitly required, not optional


\(\log z(\theta)\) in (14)
Complex log is multi-valued
Branch chosen by \(\log z(0)=0\) + continuous variation; consistent on a simply-connected angle range


Chord ≈ arc in §3.2
True only as \(d\theta\to 0\)
Higher-order terms in (17) drop out because of small-\(d\theta\) limit; the approximation is the limit itself


Direction of \(z(d\theta)-1\) is \(+i\) not \(-i\)
Sign convention dependent
Counterclockwise convention (declared up front) gives \(+i\); verified at \(\theta=\pi/2\) where \(e^{i\pi/2}=+i\)


Toby’s “binomial expansion” phrasing
Slightly imprecise; meant “power-series expansion of \(e^x\)”
Same content, correctly named

title: Proof of Euler’s Formula via Additive Functional Equation and the Logarithm Bridge
text:
Explorative directions

Next brick (Sep 27): apply Euler’s formula to solve \(\cos\theta = a\) (and even \(\cos\theta = 2\), complex case) by setting \(z = e^{i\theta}\) and reducing to a quadratic in \(z\).
Homework deferred: (i) complete the proof by writing out the determination of \(k=i\) on paper; (ii) prove the trig angle-addition formulas (1) geometrically via altitude-drop on the unit circle.
Forward pointer: all linear recursions (Jan 2026 arc) will use \(e^{i\theta}\) as the eigenfunction of the shift operator, via \(\lambda^n\) with complex \(\lambda\). Eigenvalue theory (Feb 2026) inherits this complex-rotation language.
Open question (not in class): the functional-equation proof here generalises to Banach-space valued \(z\) — the same Cauchy + log + linearise argument gives the matrix exponential \(e^{tA}\) for any square matrix \(A\). This is the bridge to the one-parameter group perspective, central to Lie theory.
Pedagogical thread: the teacher emphasised “use what you’ve already proved.” Don’t re-derive \(e^x = \sum x^n/n!\) from scratch every time. Mathematics is cumulative — secure a brick, then build on it.
Continuity at the boundary: the proof of Cauchy’s functional equation used continuity on one point (any point suffices, since shifts preserve additivity). This is the weakest hypothesis that makes the theorem work — measurability is enough; pointwise continuity at the origin is enough.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text: Three independent proofs that \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k=0\) for the \(n\)-th roots of unity (\(n\ge 2\)): (i) geometric series identity, (ii) Holmes’s concatenation as a closed regular polygon, (iii) rotational-invariance argument \(R=\omega R \Rightarrow R=0\). Then proves the order of every element divides \(\varphi(n)\) via Euclidean-division contradiction. Counts the symmetries of the square (8 = \(D_4\)) and cube (48 = \(S_4 \times \mathbb Z_2\)). Application: last three digits of \(7^{2025}\) by mod 8 + mod 125 CRT.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text: Three independent proofs that \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k=0\) for the \(n\)-th roots of unity (\(n\ge 2\)): (i) geometric series identity, (ii) Holmes’s concatenation as a closed regular polygon, (iii) rotational-invariance argument \(R=\omega R \Rightarrow R=0\). Then proves the order of every element divides \(\varphi(n)\) via Euclidean-division contradiction. Counts the symmetries of the square (8 = \(D_4\)) and cube (48 = \(S_4 \times \mathbb Z_2\)). Application: last three digits of \(7^{2025}\) by mod 8 + mod 125 CRT.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text:
Symbol dictionary







Symbol
Meaning




\(\omega\)
primitive \(n\)-th root of unity, \(\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}\)


\(\omega^k\)
the \(k\)-th \(n\)-th root of unity, \(0\le k\le n-1\)


\(R\)
the sum vector \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k\) in \(\mathbb C\)


\(\text{ord}(a)\)
order of \(a\) in \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)^\times\): smallest \(d\ge 1\) with \(a^d\equiv 1\)


\(\varphi(n)\)
Euler totient (defined Sep 13 morning)


\(D_n\)
dihedral group of the regular \(n\)-gon, \(\lvert D_n\rvert = 2n\)


\(|G|\)
order (cardinality) of group \(G\)


\(S_n\)
symmetric group on \(n\) letters, \(|S_n|=n!\)

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text:
Primitive notions and assumptions
We work in the complex plane \(\mathbb C\) with the standard polar form. Convention: angles measured counterclockwise from positive real axis; \(\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}\) is the rotation by exactly one \(n\)-th of a full turn. Imported lemmas: (i) Euler’s formula \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\) (to be proven Sep 20 — used here as imported); (ii) the geometric series identity \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} z^k = (z^n-1)/(z-1)\) for \(z\ne 1\) (algebraic; one-line proof by multiplying out); (iii) Fermat / Euler from Sep 13 morning.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text:
Part I — Three proofs that \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k = 0\)

1.1 Statement

(Sum of \(n\)-th roots of unity). For \(n \ge 2\): \[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k \;=\; 0 \qquad \text{where } \omega = e^{2\pi i/n}. \tag{1}
\]



1.2 Derivation A — Geometric series (one line)
\(\omega^n = e^{2\pi i} = 1\) by Euler’s formula, so \(\omega\) is a root of \(z^n - 1\). For \(\omega \ne 1\) (i.e. \(n\ge 2\)): \[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k = \frac{\omega^n - 1}{\omega - 1} = \frac{0}{\omega - 1} = 0. \quad\blacksquare \tag{2}
\]
Weakest step: the division in (2) is only valid because \(\omega \ne 1\); the case \(n=1\) (where \(\omega=1\) and the sum is trivially 1) is correctly excluded by the hypothesis \(n\ge 2\).


1.3 Derivation B — Rotational invariance (the symmetry argument)
Let \(R := \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^k \in \mathbb C\). Multiply both sides by \(\omega\): \[
\omega R = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^{k+1} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\omega^{j} = \omega^n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1}\omega^j = 1 + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1}\omega^j. \tag{3}
\] The right side of (3) is just \(\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\omega^j = R\) with indices shifted by one. Hence: \[
\omega R = R \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (\omega - 1) R = 0. \tag{4}
\] Since \(\omega \ne 1\), \(R = 0\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Weakest step: the shift-of-indices step in (3) is the load-bearing move; it works because multiplication by \(\omega\) permutes the set \(\{1,\omega,\dots,\omega^{n-1}\}\) — exactly the geometric image of the multiplication-permutation argument from Sep 13 morning.


1.4 Derivation C — Concatenation as a closed polygon (Holmes’s argument)
Think of each \(\omega^k\) as a unit vector. Place them tip-to-tail. The angle between consecutive vectors (after concatenation) is constant: each next vector is the previous one rotated by \(2\pi/n\). After \(n\) steps, the cumulative rotation is \(2\pi\), returning to the original direction. The resulting path is a closed regular \(n\)-gon, hence the total displacement (= the sum) is zero. \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Weakest step: “the path closes” relies on the fact that turning by \(2\pi/n\) at each of \(n\) steps brings the heading back to where it started — true by elementary trigonometry, but the geometric closure of the path requires the side lengths to be uniform (they are, all = 1) and the turning angle constant. Both hold; the argument is rigorous but appeals to geometric intuition for the polygon-closure step.


1.5 Comparison

A is the shortest and most algebraic. Imports the geometric series identity (one-line lemma).
B is the most structurally illuminating — it makes the connection to group symmetry (`multiplication by \(\omega\) is a bijection’) explicit. It is also the proof shape that generalises: e.g., for any character \(\chi\) of a finite group \(G\) with \(\chi \ne 1\), \(\sum_g \chi(g) = 0\).
C is the most visual and easiest to teach. The polygon-closure step is essentially Cavalieri-flavour reasoning; rigorous but visual.

All three give the same result and pairwise reinforce each other. A is most airtight; C is the most pedagogically valuable.


1.6 Verification

\(n=4\): \(1 + i + (-1) + (-i) = 0\). ✓
\(n=3\): \(1 + e^{2\pi i/3} + e^{4\pi i/3} = 1 + (-\tfrac12 + \tfrac{\sqrt 3}{2}i) + (-\tfrac12 - \tfrac{\sqrt 3}{2}i) = 0\). ✓
\(n=19\) (in-class example): 19 is prime; the 19 vectors are spaced \(\approx 18.95°\) apart, none diametrically opposite, yet sum to 0. The pair-up-by-conjugates trick fails to show this directly (Holmes’s first attempt) — it only proves the imaginary part is 0. Derivation B is the cleanest way to see the real part is also 0.
\(n=5\) (regular pentagon): no diametric pairs at all, yet sum = 0 by Derivation C (closed pentagon).

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
section: Part II — Order of an element divides \(\varphi(n)\)
text:
Part II — Order of an element divides \(\varphi(n)\)

2.1 Statement

(Order divides totient). For any \(a\) with \(\gcd(a,n)=1\), \(\text{ord}_n(a) \mid \varphi(n)\). \(\tag{5}\)



2.2 Derivation — Euclidean division contradiction
Let \(r = \text{ord}_n(a)\), so \(a^r \equiv 1 \pmod n\) and \(r\) is minimal. By Euler (Sep 13 AM), \(a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1\). Write \[
\varphi(n) = qr + s, \qquad 0 \le s < r \tag{6}
\] (Euclidean division). Then \[
a^{\varphi(n)} = (a^r)^q \cdot a^s \equiv 1^q \cdot a^s = a^s \pmod n. \tag{7}
\] Combining with \(a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1\): \[
a^s \equiv 1 \pmod n. \tag{8}
\] But \(0\le s < r\) and \(r\) is the smallest positive exponent with \(a^r\equiv 1\). The only consistent value is \(s = 0\), hence \(r \mid \varphi(n)\). \(\quad\blacksquare\)
Weakest step: the minimality of \(r\) in the definition of order is doing the work in step (8). If \(r\) were merely “an exponent that works” rather than the smallest, the conclusion would fail. This is why the proof is by contradiction on \(s\ne 0\).


2.3 Verification on \(n=18\)
\(\varphi(18) = 18\cdot(1-\tfrac12)(1-\tfrac13) = 6\). Compute orders in \((\mathbb Z/18\mathbb Z)^\times = \{1,5,7,11,13,17\}\):

All orders \(\in \{1,2,3,6\}\) = divisors of 6 = \(\varphi(18)\). ✓
Trick spotted in class: \(5\cdot 7 = 35 \equiv -1 \pmod{18}\). So \(5^3 = ?\): directly \(125 = 6\cdot 18 + 17 \equiv -1\), so \(5^6 \equiv 1\) but \(5^3 \equiv -1 \ne 1\), confirming \(\text{ord}(5)=6\). And \(7^3 \equiv (5\cdot 7)^3 / 5^3 \equiv (-1)^3 / (-1) = 1\), confirming \(\text{ord}(7)=3\). ✓
Definition. \(a\) is a primitive root mod \(n\) iff \(\text{ord}_n(a) = \varphi(n)\) — i.e., it achieves the maximum cycle length. Above: \(5\) and \(11\) are primitive roots mod 18.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
section: Part III — Last three digits of \(7^{2025}\) (homework warmup)
text:
Part III — Last three digits of \(7^{2025}\) (homework warmup)

3.1 Setup
Last three digits = mod 1000. \(\varphi(1000) = 1000\cdot(1-\tfrac12)(1-\tfrac15) = 400\). Since \(\gcd(7,1000)=1\), Euler: \(7^{400}\equiv 1\), so \(7^{2025} = 7^{5\cdot 400 + 25} \equiv 7^{25} \pmod{1000}\).


3.2 Derivation A — Binomial \((50-1)^{12}\) in mod 1000
\(7^{25} = 7\cdot 49^{12} = 7\cdot(50-1)^{12}\). Expand keeping only \(50^0\) and \(50^1\) (since \(50^2 = 2500 \equiv 500\), which is not zero mod 1000 — and its coefficient \(\binom{12}{2}=66\) is even, so \(66\cdot 500 = 33000 \equiv 0\), but \(50^3 = 125000 \equiv 0\) for sure):
Lower-order terms only: \[
(50-1)^{12} \equiv (-1)^{12} + \binom{12}{1}\cdot 50\cdot(-1)^{11} + \binom{12}{2}\cdot 50^2\cdot(-1)^{10} \pmod{1000}. \tag{9}
\] \[
\equiv 1 - 600 + 66\cdot 2500 = 1 - 600 + 165000 \equiv 1 - 600 + 0 = -599 \equiv 401 \pmod{1000}. \tag{10}
\] (Since \(66\cdot 2500 = 165000\), and \(165000 / 1000 = 165\) exactly, so \(\equiv 0\).)
Hence \(7^{25} \equiv 7\cdot 401 = 2807 \equiv 807 \pmod{1000}\).


3.3 Derivation B — CRT into mod 8 + mod 125
\(1000 = 8\cdot 125\), coprime.
Mod 8: \(7\equiv -1\), so \(7^{25} \equiv -1 \equiv 7 \pmod 8\).
Mod 125: \(\varphi(125) = 100\), so \(7^{100}\equiv 1\) but 25 < 100, no reduction. Use binomial: \(7^{25} = 7\cdot(50-1)^{12}\) mod 125. Since \(50^2 = 2500 = 20\cdot 125\), \(50^2\equiv 0\pmod{125}\): \[
(50-1)^{12} \equiv 1 - 600 \equiv 1 - (4\cdot 125 + 100) \equiv -100 \equiv 25 \pmod{125}. \tag{11}
\] So \(7^{25} \equiv 7\cdot 25 = 175 \equiv 50 \pmod{125}\).
Wait — let me cross-check (11). \(-600 \pmod{125}\): \(600 = 4\cdot 125 + 100\), so \(-600 \equiv -100 \equiv 25 \pmod{125}\). Then \(1+25 = 26\), so \((50-1)^{12}\equiv 26\), and \(7\cdot 26 = 182 \equiv 57 \pmod{125}\).
(Transcript correction.) The in-class teacher computed \(7\cdot 26 = 182 \equiv 57 \pmod{125}\) — which matches my recomputation. So \(7^{25} \equiv 57 \pmod{125}\), not 50.
CRT recombination. Find \(x\in\{0,\dots,999\}\) with \(x\equiv 57\pmod{125}\) and \(x\equiv 7\pmod 8\).
Candidates \(\{57, 57+250, 57+500, 57+750\} = \{57, 307, 557, 807\}\) (skipping 250 at a time to preserve oddness). Check mod 8: \(57\bmod 8 = 1\); \(307\bmod 8 = 3\); \(557\bmod 8 = 5\); \(807\bmod 8 = 7\). ✓ So \[
7^{2025} \equiv 807 \pmod{1000}. \tag{12}
\] Last three digits: 807. Both A and B agree.


3.4 Sanity by direct cycle
From Sep 13 morning §3.4: \(7^4 \equiv 1\pmod{100}\) (order 4 mod 100). What’s the order mod 1000? Compute: \(7^4 = 2401\); \(7^{20} = (2401)^5\) — too tedious by hand. But from CRT: order mod 1000 = lcm(order mod 8, order mod 125). Order mod 8: \(7\equiv -1\), so order 2. Order mod 125: \(7\) has order dividing \(\varphi(125)=100\); from \(7^{25}\equiv 57\not\equiv 1\), order doesn’t divide 25; checking, the order is 100. So lcm\((2,100)=100\). Thus \(7\) has order 100 mod 1000, and \(7^{2025} = 7^{20\cdot 100 + 25} \equiv 7^{25} \equiv 807 \pmod{1000}\). ✓

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
section: Part IV — Counting symmetries of square and cube
text:
Part IV — Counting symmetries of square and cube

4.1 Square: \(|D_4| = 8\)
Definition. A symmetry of a labelled figure is a geometric operation (rotation, reflection, or composition) that preserves the adjacency relationship of vertices.
Direct enumeration. Rotations by \(0°, 90°, 180°, 270°\) (= 4 rotations). Reflections across 4 axes (2 through opposite vertices, 2 through edge midpoints). Total: 8. ✓
Combinatorial count. Place vertex labelled “1” in any of 4 corners (4 choices). Place “2” in either of the two neighbours of “1” (2 choices: clockwise or counterclockwise). Remaining vertices are then forced. Total: \(4\times 2 = 8\). ✓


4.2 Cube: 48 symmetries
Combinatorial count. Place “1” at any of 8 vertices. Place “2” at any of its 3 neighbours. Place “5” (the vertex opposite to one of the remaining two neighbours of vertex 1, chosen as anchor) — 2 choices. Remaining 4 vertices forced. Total: \(8\times 3\times 2 = 48\). ✓
Why direct enumeration is hard. The teacher reports counting \(36\) or \(42\) on first attempts; the missing symmetries are the turn-flips (improper rotations: composition of a rotation with a reflection through the cube’s centre). These are not easily visualised — Wikipedia’s animation is recommended.
Group structure (forward pointer). The 48 symmetries form a group \(\cong S_4 \times \mathbb Z_2\): - \(S_4\) (\(|S_4|=24\)) acts as the rotation group of the cube (= permutation group on the 4 long diagonals). - \(\mathbb Z_2\) is the central inversion (point reflection through centre). - \(24\times 2 = 48\). ✓

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
section: Part V — Connection between modular and geometric symmetry
text:
Part V — Connection between modular and geometric symmetry
The three threads of this lesson are one structure:








Symmetry source
Operation
Invariance




\((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)^\times\), multiplication by \(a\)
permutes the unit set
\(\prod x_i\) is invariant (Fermat/Euler)


\(n\)-th roots of unity, multiplication by \(\omega\)
rotation, permutes the vector set
\(\sum \omega^k\) is invariant ⇒ \(=0\)


Square/cube, geometric operations
permute the vertex set
the figure is invariant



Connection arrow. Derivation B of (1) is the exact geometric analogue of Derivation A of Fermat (Sep 13 morning, eq (2)–(4)). Both invoke: “multiplication by an element permutes a finite set; therefore the sum/product is fixed.” This is the start of the representation theory of finite groups — the unifying language for symmetry in algebra and geometry.

title: Roots of Unity Sum to Zero by Symmetry; Order Divides φ(n); Symmetries of Square and Cube
text:
Unified picture
\[\boxed{\text{Finite group } G \xrightarrow{\text{acts on itself}} \text{ permutation } \xrightarrow{\text{sum / product invariant}} \text{numerical identity}}\]

Multiplication mod \(n\)
